Regional Water Program 
The Regional Water Program, projected to comprise approximately $1.260 billion of the 2026 Capital Plan, 
finances capital improvements to the Regional Water System. Regional Water Program capital investments include, 
but are n ot limited to, water treatment, water transmission, water supply and storage, watersheds and land 
management, communication and monitoring, and building and grounds management.  
The Regional Water category of the 2026 Capital Plan also includes approximately $60.2 million in funding 
for project planning related to certain regional projects in the AWS Program, as well as for additional planning efforts 79 
for other alternative water supply options . For more information regarding the AWS Program, see “ – Water Supply 
Initiatives – AWS Program.” 
Local Water Program 
The Local Water Program, projected to comprise approximately $1.158 billion of the 2026 Capital Plan , 
finances capital improvements to the In -City Distribution System. The primary component of the Local W ater 
Program, projected to comprise approximately $755 million, is the local Water Conveyance and Distribution program, 
which consists of pipeline installation, repair and replacement.  For pipeline replacement, the SFPUC has replaced an 
average of 7.8 miles of pipe annually over the past five years compared to a previous rate of four to six miles per year. 
The SFPUC has increased its planned investment in the 2026 Capital Plan in order to increase the pipeline replacement 
rate to approximately 12 miles per year. 
The Local Water category of the 2026 Capital Plan  also includes planning efforts to advance conservation 
and develop local water supply sources. For more information about these local water supply initiatives, see “– Water 
Supply Initiatives – Local Water Supply Initiatives.” 
Hetch Hetchy Water 
General. Upgrades to the aging facilities of Hetch Hetchy Water and Power are being constructed to ensure 
reliability and preparedness for the future. The Hetch Hetchy Water category of the 2026 Capital Plan is projected to 
cost approximately $ 625.8 million. Upcountry water and power facilities being assessed and rehabilitated, where 
needed, include three impounding reservoirs, three regulating reservoirs, three large powerhouses, one small 
powerhouse, two switchyards, three substations, 170 miles of pipeline and tunnels, almost 100  miles of paved road, 
over 160 miles of transmission lines, as well as watershed land and rights-of-way property. The Water Enterprise will 
fund all assets relating to Hetch Hetchy Water (consisting of approximately $187.8 million of Hetch Hetchy Water 
costs) and the Hetch Hetchy Water portion of jointly- owned assets of Hetch Hetchy Water and the Power Enterprise 
in the Hetch Hetchy Water and Power System (consisting of approxima tely $438.0 million of Hetch Hetchy Water 
costs). See “THE PUBLIC UTILITIES COMMISSION – Organization, Purposes and Powers – Hetch Hetchy – Water 
and Power Operations .” A significant project among the Hetch Hetchy Water category is the repair of the existi ng 
Mountain Tunnel which conveys all Tuolumne River supplies through a single conduit. See “– Mountain Tunnel.” 
Mountain Tunnel. As part of the Hetch Hetchy Regional Water System, Mountain Tunnel is a critical water 
conveyance facility. Built between 1917 and 1925, Mountain Tunnel extends 18.9  miles from the Early Intake Dam 
to Priest Reservoir. The upper 7.9 miles are unlined (natural rock) and the lower 11.0 miles are lined with concrete. 
Inspections in 1989, 2006, 2008 and 2017 identified signs of deteri oration in the lining which were projected to 
increase over time. The risk of failure of Mountain Tunnel, defined as a loss of 25% carrying capacity, is currently 
low but will increase over time. Failure of Mountain Tunnel would have a significant impact on Hetch Hetchy Water 
operations and could cause up to six months of water supply disruption. 
The SFPUC created four parallel tracks to investigate and provide repairs to Mountain Tunnel: (1)  the 
Mountain Tunnel Inspection and Interim Repairs Project, compl eted in 2017, (2)  the Mountain Tunnel Adits and 
Access Improvement Project, also completed in 2017, (3) 2018 Mountain Tunnel Interim Repairs Project, which was 
completed in 2019, and (4)  the Mountain Tunnel Improvements Project, currently in construction. A complete 
shutdown and draining of Mountain Tunnel was performed during 2017 to accomplish the Inspection and Interim 
Repairs Project and the Adits and Access Improvement Project, as well as to develop information and knowledge for 
the design and construc tion of the Long -Term Improvements Project. Additional interim repairs were performed in 
2019 to complete the most significant repairs needed to reduce the risk of failures in the concrete lining. The Mountain 
Tunnel Improvements Project, currently in construction, provides long term rehabilitation and repairs to the tunnel as 
well as the addition of flow controls (valving) on the downstream end of the tunnel near Priest Reservoir. The flow 
controls will allow the tunnel to be operated in a full state at different flow rates which will reduce lining deterioration. 
The Mountain Tunnel Improvements construction contract was awarded in October 2020, with Notice to Proceed in 
January 2021. Significant project work has been completed to date including construction of an access shaft for a new 
flow control facility near Priest Reservoir, construction of new access adit tunnel near Priest Reservoir, partial repair 
of tunnel interior lining, access road improvements, and various improvements along the tunnel alignment.  The 
current construction contract is forecast to achieve substantial completion at the end of 2026. However, some scope 80 
of work related to the tunnel and roadway improvements at the South Fork site are planned to be removed from the 
current contract and moved to a future contract due to differences of opinion about constructability with the current 
contractor. The City is evaluating alternative delivery procurement methods to complete the remaining work. While 
the majority of the tunnel improvements will be completed, delaying this work will delay the full use of the flow 
control facility until these repairs can be performed.  
Improvements to Mountain Tunnel are projected to cost a total of approximately $268.7 million; 
$210.1 million has been appropriated to the project to date, and an additional $21.4  million will be appropriated for 
the 2026 Capital Plan. The portion of the project that performs repairs and improvements to the Mountain Tunnel is 
projected to cost $134.3  million; the tunnel has been cla ssified as a Water asset and thus these costs will be funded 
100% by Water Enterprise. The remaining portion of the project that constructs the new flow control facility is 
projected to cost $134.3 million; because this flow control facility will benefit b oth water delivery and power 
production, it has been classified as a joint asset, and thus will be funded by Water Enterprise and Power Enterprise, 
pursuant to existing cost-sharing agreements with wholesale customers. The current cost sharing agreement al locates 
45% of the cost for joint assets to the Water Enterprise and 55% of the cost to the Power Enterprise. See “FINANCIAL 
OPERATIONS – General – Allocation of Hetch Hetchy Project Costs.”  
The SFPUC has entered into the DWSRF Loan for up to $239.219 mil lion with the SWRCB to finance a 
portion of the cost of the Mountain Tunnel Project pursuant to the SWRCB’s Drinking Water State Revolving Fund 
Program. For more information  regarding the loan, see “OBLIGATIONS PAYABLE FROM REVENUES – Parity 
State Loans.” 
Water System Improvement Program 
General. The Water System Improvement Program is a $4.793 billion program that consists of 87 capital 
projects to repair, replace and upgrade critical portions of the Regional Water System and the Local Water System to 
meet specific level of service goals and objectives for seismic reliability, delivery reliability, water quality and water 
supply in an environmentally sustainable and cost-effective manner. Objectives for WSIP include (i) furnishing system 
improvements to provide high quality water that reliably meets current and foreseeable local, State and federal 
requirements; (ii) reducing vulnerability of the water system to damage from earthquakes; (iii) increasing reliability 
of the system to deliver water by improving redundancy needed to accommodate planned outages for maintenance 
and unplanned outages resulting from facility failure; (iv)  providing near-term improvement of water supply/drought 
protection; (v)  setting forth long- term water supply/drought management options for technical evaluation, cost 
analysis and environmental review; (vi) enhancing sustainability through improvements that optimize protection of 
the natural and human environment; and (vii)  providing improvements resulting in a cost -effective, fully-operational 
water system. As of December 2024, only two WSIP projects remain to be completed: (i) the Regional Groundwater 
Storage and Recovery Project, and (ii)  the Alameda Creek Recapture Project. Both projects are currently in the 
construction phase and are further described below. 
Schedule and Budget . In 2005, the SFPUC approved the original scope, schedule and a budget of 
$4.342 billion for WSIP (the “2005 Baseline Budget”). Since then, the SFPUC has approved several revisions to the 
schedule and budget for WSIP, most recently in April 2024 (the “ 2024 Budget ”). The variance between the 
2005 Baseline Budget and the 2024 Budget of $4.793 billion is an increase of approximately $446 million, or a 10.3% 
increase and is due mainly to construction cost i ncreases to the WSIP’s largest project, the Calaveras Dam 
Replacement Project (which increased by approximately $308 million, or 118% of total project cost). All 
appropriations for the WSIP have been made and WSIP is consequently not included in the 2026 C apital Plan (as 
defined herein). Pursuant to the 2024 Budget, the SFPUC anticipates overall program completion in June 2032.  
Regional Groundwater Storage and Recovery Project . The Regional Groundwater Storage and Recovery 
Project has been divided into two phases. The Phase 1 construction contract, which includes the construction of up to 
16 new groundwater recovery wells and test wells, chemical treatment equipment, pumping systems and pipelines, 
and other associated facilities, was completed in September 2 022 and closed out in January 2023. Phase  2 has been 
divided into two construction contracts (Phases 2A and 2B); Phase 2 will provide improvements to the wells installed 
under Phase 1 to provide corrosion protection and other operational improvements, as well as to complete installation 
and connection of the South San Francisco Main Well. The Phase 2A contract to install cathodic protection and other 
improvements to some of the wells started construction in June 2022 and is anticipated to be completed later in 2025.  81 
The Phase 2B contract for installation of the South San Francisco Main Well facility and pipeline started construction 
in 2024 and is anticipated to be completed in 2026. 
Alameda Creek Recapture Project. The Alameda Creek Recapture Project includes construction of facilities 
in and around an existing quarry pit in Sunol Valley to recapture water that will be released from Calaveras Reservoir 
and/or bypassed around the Alameda Creek Diversion Dam during future operations of Calaveras Reservoir. 
Construction of the facility started in June 2021. In April 2023, the SFPUC terminated the project’s construction 
contract due to concerns regarding worsening pond slope erosion, anticipated facility operating and maintenance 
complexity, and excessive change orders to redesign the facility to accommodate the erosive slope conditions. The 
project is being re -evaluated for short-term and long-term slope stability remediation and to consider improvements 
for operation and maintenance sustainability. The SFPUC will work with the quarry operator to stabilize the pond 
banks and re-evaluate the facility design to simplify operation and maintenance requirements. It is anticipated that a 
new construction contract will be issued using a design/build project delivery method and explore the possibility of 
adding initial operation and maintenance scope as part of the contract. The project is anticipated to be completed in 
2032.  
Water Supply Initiatives 
AWS Program 
General. W
ith WSIP nearing completion, there are two main drivers for the SFPUC’s future infrastructure 
water needs: (i) environmental flow requirements on the Tuolumne River that could result in a reduction of available 
water supplies by approximately 105 mgd during dry years at the current contract levels of demand if the 2018 Plan 
Amendment (as defined herein) requiring the release of 40% of unimpaired flow in the Tuolumne River is 
implemented (see “REGULATORY MATTERS – Bay Delta Water Quality Standards”); and (i i) a policy decision 
before the SFPUC on whether to provide a permanent supply guarantee of 9 mgd to the cities of San José and 
Santa Clara, who are currently interruptible customers (see “ – Wholesale Deliveries  – Status of San José and 
Santa Clara” and “– 2028 Water Supply Decisions”). Both drivers may cause additional dry-year shortages. In 2019, 
with the goal of ensuring that the water needs of Retail and Wholesale Customers will continue to be met under future 
conditions, the SFPUC established the AWS Program to evaluate new and diverse alternative water supply options, 
such as expanding storage, groundwater banking, transfers, purified water (potable reuse), desalination and 
technological innovations.  
Planning for alternative water supplies requires a c
 omprehensive and detailed evaluation effort that considers 
several interrelated challenges, many of which are different than those faced through traditional water supply planning 
at the SFPUC. These planning challenges include complex multi -party partnerships and approaches for integrating 
new supply sources into an expansive and largely homogenous water system. Additionally, as with any long- term 
water supply planning effort, the SFPUC must also contend with uncertainties such as new and prospective 
regulations, climate change and extreme weather events and long -term demand projections. To minimize the risk of 
overbuilding, the SFPUC’s approach involves planning for existing and potential obligations and advancing projects 
toward implementation based on projected demands on the Regional Water System. The approach is necessarily 
dynamic as both supply-side and demand-side drivers continue to change over time. As planning continues, whether 
individual projects proceed to construction will depend on their tec hnical, financial, and institutional feasibility, as 
well as changes that could affect the drivers of the projected shortages. 
AWS Program Planning Priorities and Level of Service Goals and Objectives . T
 he planning priorities of 
the AWS Program include (i) meeting regulatory requirements including instream flow releases, (ii)  meeting existing 
obligations to existing permanent customers, (iii) determining whether the SFPUC will have long-term water supplies 
available to make current interruptible customers permanent, and (iv) meeting increased demands of existing and 
interruptible customers. In conjunction with these planning priorities, the SFPUC considers the level of service goals 
and objections related to water supply and sustainability when planning for new water supply opportunities. The key 
level of service goals and objectives relevant to AWS Program planning include (a)  meeting dry-year delivery needs 
while limiting rationing to a maximum 20% system -wide reduction in water service during extended droughts; 
(b) diversifying water supply options during non-drought and drought periods; (c) improving use of new water sources 
and drought management, including groundwater, recycled water, conservation, and transfers; (d)  meeting, at a 82 
minimum, all current and anticipated legal requirements for protection of fish and wildlife habitat; and (e)  providing 
operational flexibility. 
AWS Projects. I n 2019, the SFPUC identified six regional alternative water supply options under the AWS 
Program. The SFPUC has been studying the feasibility of these projects. To date, two regional projects remain active, 
three projects have been deferred, and one project has been cancelled, each as further described below. Because of the 
inherent technical, institutional and operational complexities, AWS projects are anticipated to be more expensive than 
traditional SFPUC capital projects. The 2026 Capital Plan includes approximately $60.2 million for project planning 
related to the active projects described below, as well as for addition al planning efforts for other alternative water 
supply options.  
PureWater Peninsula (formerly Crystal Springs Purified Water)(A
 ctive): This project could provide 6-8 mgd 
of water supply through reservoir water augmentation at Crystal Springs Reservoir and an additional 4-6 mgd (for a 
total of up to 12 mgd) through direct potable reuse to other partner agencies. Treated wastewater from Silicon Valley 
Clean Water and the City of San Mateo would go through a water purification process that uses multi-barrier treatment 
technology to meet State and federal drinking water quality standards. The purified water would be transmitted over 
12 miles to Crystal Springs Reservoir and/or other neighboring water service areas. Water conveyed to Crystal Springs 
Reservoir would be blended with regional surface water supplies and treated again at Harry Tracy Water Treatment 
Plant. An alternative that delivers up to 12 mgd directly into the SFPUC’s treated water delivery system was also 
evaluated and the two project alternatives are currently being reviewed by the SFPUC. In addition to the SFPUC and 
the wastewater agencies, Cal Water, the City of Redwood City, Mid-Peninsula Water District and BAWSCA are also 
participating in this project. 
South Bay Purified Water (A
 ctive): As described above under “ – General,” one of the main drivers for the 
SFPUC’s future infrastructure water needs is a policy decision before the SFPUC regarding whether to change the 
status of the cities of San José and Santa  Clara from interruptible to permanent customers  (see “THE WATER 
ENTERPRISE – Wholesale Deliveries – Status of San José and Santa Clara” and “ – 2028 Water Supply”). To help 
offset the potential incremental dry year impact of adding new obligations and support their request for permanent 
status, San José and Santa  Clara are partnering with the SFPUC to evaluate a new water supply project  that could 
provide 3.5 mgd of dry year supply to the SFPUC. Additionally, this project would produce 6.5 mgd of supply in all 
years for use by S an José and Santa Clara. Initial feasibility of the project was completed in July 2023. Discussions 
regarding agreements are ongoing and additional technical analysis will be needed before project planning is 
concluded.  
Daly City Recycled Water Expansion (Deferred) : Thi
 s project was intended to produce approximately 
1.0 mgd of recycled water on an average annual basis, resulting in approximately 0.7 mgd of additional dry-year 
potable supply availability in the South Westside Groundwater Basin. The primary purpose of this project was to 
provide recycled water for certain cemetery and irrigation use, offsetting existing groundwater pumping from the 
South Westside Groundwater Basin, and thereby increasing the reliability of the basin for additional drinking wa ter 
supply during droughts for SFPUC customers. This project has been deferred due to, among other reasons, high capital 
cost requirements and reduced water supply benefits to the SFPUC, and may be reevaluated in the future.  
Alameda County Water District – Un
 ion Sanitary District Purified Water Partnership (Deferred): This project 
was intended to provide Alameda County Water District (“ ACWD”) with a new purified water supply by treating 
wastewater from Union Sanitary District (“ USD”) to meet drinking water standards  and transmitting such purified 
water to the Quarry Lakes Groundwater Recharge Area to supplement recharge into the Niles Cone Groundwater 
Basin or for other uses in the ACWD service area. The first phase of the project was intended to provide ACWD with 
up to 5.4 mgd of additional water supply, enabling an in- lieu exchange with the SFPUC that would preserve more 
water in SFPUC’s Regional Water System. This project has been deferred due to, among other reasons, relatively high 
capital cost requireme nts and USD and ACWD pursuing other, higher -priority planning efforts, and may be 
reevaluated in the future. 
Calaveras Reservoir Expansion (Deferred): Thi s project was intended to add up to an additional 289,000 
acre-feet, or 94 billion gallons, of water s torage to the Calaveras Dam (which currently has a water storage capacity 
of 96,850 acre-feet, or 31 billion gallons) that could be available in all water year types. This project included raising 
the dam, expanding the capacity of the outlet structures and spillway, and adding necessary transmission and pumping 83 
infrastructure to convey water to the Calaveras Reservoir. This project has been deferred due to, among other reasons, 
high capital cost requirements, and may be reevaluated in the future. 
Los Vaqueros Reservoir Expansion (Cancelled): T his project was a storage project proposed by Contra Costa 
Water District (“CCWD”), which owns and operates the existing Los Vaqueros Reservoir located in northeastern 
Contra Costa County. CCWD  invited other water agencies in the region, including the SFPUC, to participate in the 
project, which would have enlarged the reservoir for use by the other agencies, to increase water supply reliability for 
customers and to provide ecosystem benefits to south- of-Delta wildlife refuges and Delta fisheries. The SFPUC 
contemplated storage space in the project to provide a dry- year water supply benef it to the SFPUC’s customers; 
however, CCWD cancelled the project in fall 2024 due to, among other reasons,  high capital cost requirements, 
reduced water supply benefits, and other project risks. 
San Joaquin V alley Projects 
I
n addition to the AWS Program described above, the SFPUC is also exploring opportunities for collaboration 
in the San Joaquin Valley that could reduce potential future water supply shortfalls in the SFPUC service area. These 
three concepts are described below. 
Groundwater Banking.  G
 roundwater banking in the Modesto Irrigation District and Turlock Irrigation 
District service areas could be used to provide some additional water supply to meet instream releases in dry years 
reducing water supply impacts to the SFPUC service area. For example, additional surface water could be provided 
to irrigators in wet years, which would off-set the use of groundwater, thereby allowing the groundwater to remain in 
the basin rather than be consumptively used. The groundwater that remains in the basin can then be used in a 
subsequent dry year for irrigation, subsequently freeing up surface water that would have otherwise been delivered to 
irrigators to meet instream flow requirements. 
Inter-Basin Collaborations . I
 nter-Basin collaborations could include establishing a partnership between 
interests on the Tuolumne River and those on the Stanislaus River, which would allow responsibility for streamflow 
to be assigned variably based on the annual hydrology. The Tuolumne system tends to spill more excess flow in wetter 
years than the Stanislaus system, and this excess flow could be shaped and credited to meet Stanislaus system 
requirements, while New Melones Reservoir in the Stanislaus system is refilling. Stored water could then be partially 
used to provide required streamflow to meet Stanislaus and Tuolumne requirements in future dry years. 
Dry Year Water Transfer. Th
 e SFPUC has initiated discussions with Oakdale Irrigation District to transfer 
2 mgd (2,240 acre-feet) from Oakdale Irrigation District in dry years only. While no transfer has been secured to date, 
the SFPUC continues to explore potential transfer opportunities on the Tuolumne River and throughout the San 
Joaquin Valley. 
Local Water Supply Initiatives 
T
he SFPUC is also continually expanding efforts to advance conservation and develop local supply sources. 
These ongoing local programs are described below. 
Local Water Conservation.  The SFPUC maintains a robust water conservation program and has one of the 
lowest rates of per capita water use in California. Conservation programs include rebates and incentives for 
replacement of inefficient plumbing fixtures, appliances and equipment ; water-wise evaluations; free water-saving 
devices; tools to help customers understand and manage their water use; education; and outreach. 
Onsite Water Reuse . Th
 e Onsite Water Reuse Program allows for the collection, treatment and use of 
alternate water sources for non-potable applications in individual buildings and at the district -scale. The SFPUC also 
administers the Non-Potable Water Ordinance that requires onsite water reuse systems for new development projects 
of 100,000 gross square feet or greater in San Francisco, and the Stormwater Management Ordinance that outlines the 
mandatory requirements for managing post -construction stormwater runoff and provides guidance on how to 
incorporate green infrastructure into site design. 84 
Ideation to Implementation (I2I) Program :  This program helps the SFPUC better align and overcome 
challenges that can affect successful implementation of new water supply and conservation projects. These challenges 
may be related to regulations, resource availability, climate uncertainty, or affordability. The I2I Program encourages 
creativity, collaboration, and opportunities for partnership in addressing implementation challenges. Examples of the 
I2I Program ’s work that have been executed are demonstrations of new technologies and grant funds to support 
partnership opportunities for applications such as piloting atmospheric water generation technology, supporting heat 
recovery in non-potable systems, testing leak detection, and developing guidance for breweries treating process water 
for reuse.  
PureWaterSF: Thi
 s program aims to plan for the phased development of  a new local drinking water supply 
in San Francisco of approximately 2 -8 mgd through the treatment of recycled water from the Oceanside Water 
Pollution Control Plant (PureWaterSF – West) and the Southeast Water Pollution Control Plant (PureWaterSF – East). 
From 2018-2020, the SFPUC completed a temporary small -scale research and demonstration purified water (potable 
reuse) project at its headquarters. In 2022, the SFPUC completed an evaluation of four alternative scenarios for 
purified water development. The SFPUC is currently reviewing treatability and developing preliminary design for an 
initial phase of the PureWaterSF – East project. The initial phase includes the planning and construction of a purified 
water public engagement and operator training facility . While the total cost of  this program has not been determined 
and requires further analysis, the SFPUC anticipates significant capital expenditures will be necessary for full 
implementation. 
Environmental Planning Considerations 
P
rojects undertaken by the SFPUC are subject to CEQA and certain projects involving the participation of 
federal agencies, including projects on federal land, are also subject to the National Environmental Policy Act 
(“NEPA”). The San Francisco Planning Department (the “Planning Department”), acting as the CEQA lead agency 
under Chapter 31 of the City’s Administrative Code (“Chapter 31”), generally coordinates CEQA review of SFPUC 
projects. The SFPUC also coordinates with federal NEPA lead agencies to prepare NEPA documentation for SFPUC 
projects. 
Under CEQA, a project that may have a significant effect on the environment and is to be carried out or 
approved by a public agency must comply with a comprehensive environmental review process, including the 
preparation of an environmental impact report (“ EIR”). The EIR reflects not only an independent technical analysis 
of the project’s potential environmental impacts, but also the comments of other agencies with some form of 
jurisdiction over the project and the comments of interested members of the public. Contents of the EIR include a 
detailed statement of the project’s significant environmental effects; any such effects that cannot be avoided if the 
project is implemented; mitigation measures proposed to minimize such effects; alternatives to the proposed project; 
the relationship between short -term and long -term impacts; any significant irreversible environmental changes that 
would result from the project; the project’s growth-inducing impacts; and a brief statement setting forth the agency’s 
reasons for determining that certain effects are not significant and hence do not require discussion in the EIR. Before 
approving a project, the SFPUC must make findings on whether or how it can mitigate the significant environmental 
effects of the project. If the project requires mitigation, the SFPUC must adopt a mitigation monitoring and reporting 
program on how the mitigation is carried out during project implementation. If a project’s significant effects can be 
mitigated, the SFPUC can adopt a written statement (called a mitigated negative declaration) or if the project will not 
have a significant effect on the environment, the SFPUC may adopt a written statement (called a negative declaration) 
to that effect and in both cases need not prepare an EIR. After deciding to approve or carry out a project, either 
following the EIR process or after adopting a mitigated negative declaration or a negative declaration, the Planning 
Department must file a notice of such determination with the Office of the County Clerk. CEQA also contains several 
exemptions, which the SFPUC uses for its projects when appropriate. 
Any action or proceeding challenging the Planning Department determination must be brought within 30 
days following the filing of such notice. Actions have been, and in the future may be, filed challenging a project’s 
compliance with CEQA, including the adequacy of the EIR and other environmental documents for particular projects. 
If an action challenging the SFPUC’s compliance with CEQA is successful, the particular project could be delayed, 
revised, suspended or canceled.  85 
As part of its regular planning and budgetary process, the SFPUC gives careful attention to environmental 
considerations. All projects are evaluated under the SFPUC’s environmental evaluation procedures  (Infrastructure 
Procedures 7.01 and 7.02), developed in compliance with federal and State laws and regulations, City ordinances and 
the City’s Administrative Code procedures, including Chapter 31. 
Prior to the sale of new money bonds, the Planning Departm ent will issue a “Planning Certificate” required 
under Proposition E. The Planning Certificate will identify the status of environmental review for each capital project 
to be funded under the proposed bond sale and the type of CEQA document either completed or to be completed for 
each project. CEQA compliance must be completed for each project prior to project approval or approval to award a 
construction contract to implement any project to be funded by the proposed bond sale.  No Planning Certificate is 
required prior to the sale of the 2025ABC Bonds. 
FINANCING OF CAPITAL IMPROVEMENTS 
Long Term Financing of Capital Program 
The Water Enterprise’s 10-year financial plan for Fiscal Years 2025 -26 to 2034- 35 (the “ 2026 Financial 
Plan”) was adopted by the Commission on February 11, 2025. The 2026 Financial Plan is based on the 2026 Capital 
Plan. Pursuant to the 2026 Financial Plan, long- term debt financing, comprised of additional Bonds and State 
Revolving Fund loans, is projected to fund approximately $1.908 billion of the 2026 Capital Plan, and revenue (pay -
as-you-go) funding is projected to fund approximately $1.134 billion of the 2026 Capital Plan. See “OBLIGATIONS 
PAYABLE FROM REVENUES – Parity State Loans.” 
The SFPUC’s long-term capital and financial planning is performed on an annual rolling 10- year forward 
looking basis. Although the 2026 Financial Plan currently contemplates the issuance of additional Bonds and State 
Revolving Fund loans to finance the capital program, the SFPUC may also consider additional financing options  in 
the future, including but not limited to other parity or subordinate federal and State loans.  
Interim Funding Program Facilities 
The SFPUC’s Interim Funding Program is a short -term financing program consisti ng of a combination of 
lines of credit and credit facilities, liquidity facilities and letters of credit supporting the SFPUC’s ability to issue 
short-term indebtedness, including the issuance of Commercial Paper Notes. The SFPUC utilizes the Interim Funding 
Program to meet the expenditure and encumbrance needs of capital projects on an interim basis through design and 
into the early project construction phase. The Interim Funding Program is authorized for the Water Enterprise in the 
aggregate principal amo unt of $7 50 million. Of this amount, $ 550 million is authorized for the SFPUC to issue 
Commercial Paper Notes and the remaining $200 million is in the form of separate bank revolving credit agreements, 
each of which permits the SFPUC to make draws directly from respective bank, with the SFPUC’s payment obligation 
evidenced by the Revolving Notes.  See “OBLIGATIONS PAYABLE FROM REVENUES – Subordinate Debt and 
Interim Funding Program.” 
Interim funding program obligations are then refunded and consolidated into  either long-term revenue bond 
issues or a Parity State Loan when the outstanding and encumbered amount of the interim funding obligations 
approaches authorized limits. This approach allows the SFPUC to take advantage of historically lower short -term 
interest rates, and to size and closely time long-term financings with projected need. 
As of March  17 , 202 5, the SFPUC had Commercial Paper Notes outstanding in the aggregate principal 
amount of $310.9 million and no amount outstanding under the Revolving Notes. The SFPUC anticipates refunding 
all outstanding Commercial Paper Notes with proceeds of the 2025 New Money Bonds  anticipated to be issued in 
June 2025. See “INTRODUCTION – Outstanding and Future  Parity Bonds and Other Indebtedness .” The SFPUC 
anticipates issuing additional Commercial Paper Notes and making draws directly on the revolving credit agreements 
to provide interim financing for Water Enterprise capital projects.  86

The repayment of projected principal and interest on future debt issues has been incorporated into the 
development of the SFPUC’s approved retail water rates through Fiscal Year 2025-26, and has also been reflected in 
the remaining projection period set  forth in the 2026 Financial Plan. Pursuant to the WSA, a share of debt service 
associated with improvements to the Regional Water System is the responsibility of the Wholesale Customers. 
Pursuant to the 2026 Financial Plan, the SFPUC projects that retail water rates will increase annually by an 
average of approximately 6.2% from Fiscal Year 2025-26 to Fiscal Year 2034-35. On May 23, 2023, the Commission 
approved retail water rates and charges for the three -year period effective July 1, 2023 through June  30, 2026. 
However, no retail rate increases beyond June 30, 2026, have been proposed to, or adopted by, the Commission or 
submitted to the Board of Supervisors, and any future retail water rate increases are subject to future approval by the 
Commission, subject to the Board of Supervisors’ ability to reject rate increases. See “FINANCIAL OPERATIONS – 
Retail Water Sales Revenue.” 
FINANCIAL OPERATIONS 
General 
The SFPUC is a department of the City and, as such, the financial operations of the SFPUC’s three enterprises 
are included in the Annual Comprehensive Financial Report of the City and shown as enterprise funds. The City’ s 
Annual Comprehensive Financial Report is not incorporated by reference herein. 
The following information is provided with respect to the Water Enterprise only and does not purport to 
reflect the financial position of the SFPUC or the City as a whole. 
Basis of Accounting. The accounts of the Water Enterprise are organized based on a proprietary fund type, 
specifically an enterprise fund. The financial activities of the Water Enterprise are accounted for on a flow of economic 
resources measurement focus, using the accrual basis of accounting. Under this method, all assets and liabilities 
associated with its operations are included on the statement of net position; revenues are recorded when earned, and 
expenses are recorded when liabilities are incurred. 
The SFPUC applies all applicable GASB pronouncements. 87 
City Budget Process . The SFPUC’s operating and capital budget preparation and approval is a part of a 
City-wide process. The SFPUC is one of several departments that prepares biennial budgets. The Commission reviews 
and approves the SFPUC’s two-year budget, which is then submitted to the Mayor’s Office for review. The Mayor 
then incorporates the proposed budget, with amendments, into the City- wide budget that is submitted to the Board of 
Supervisors for approval. Under the Charter, the Board of Supervisors may increase or decrease any proposed 
expenditure in the Mayor’s budget so long as the aggregate changes do not cause the expenditures to exceed the total 
amount of expenditures proposed by the Mayor. The Charter further provides that the Mayor may reduce or reject any 
expenditure authorized by the Board of Supervisors except appropriations for bond interest, redemption or other fixed 
charges, subject to reinstatement of any such expenditure by a two-thirds vote of the Board of Supervisors. 
City Services Auditor. The City Services Auditor (“CSA”) is an audit function within the Office of the City 
Controller. The CSA has broad oversight authority and responsibilities including, but not limited to, (i) reporting upon 
level of effectiveness for City public services, (ii) auditing financial and management performance of City departments 
and functions, (iii) ensuring the financial integrity and improving the overall performance and efficiency of City 
government, and (iv) maintaining a whistleblower hotline to investigate upon reports of fraud, waste and abuse.  
Sources of Rev enue. The Water Enterprise’s principal source of revenue is the sale of water to its Retail 
Customers and wholesale customers. The setting of water rates by the SFPUC  is not subject to any State or federal 
regulatory approval. The SFPUC’s ability to generate revenue may be limited by certain provisions of the State 
Constitution and the Charter of the City. See “CONSTITUTIONAL, STATUTORY AND CHARTER 
LIMITATIONS.” 
Allocation of Hetch Hetchy Project Costs. A number of the facilities of the Hetch Hetchy Project  are joint 
assets and are used for both water transmission and power generation and transmission, benefitting both Hetch Hetchy 
Water and the Power Enterprise. All power sales revenues are allocated to the Power Enterprise. Per negotiation with 
the Wholesa le Customers reflected in the terms of the WSA, operating and capital costs benefitting the Power 
Enterprise and 55% of operating and capital costs that jointly benefit both Hetch Hetchy Water and the Power 
Enterprise are allocated to the Power Enterprise. Operating and capital costs benefitting Hetch Hetchy Water and 45% 
of operating and capital costs jointly benefitting both Hetch Hetchy Water and the Power Enterprise are allocated to 
the Water Enterprise. Costs allocated to the Water Enterprise are paid through an inter -enterprise transfer from the 
Water Enterprise to Hetch Hetchy Water and Power. Such transfers constitute “Operation and Maintenance Costs of 
the Enterprise.” See “– Operating and Maintenance Expenses – Inter-Enterprise Transfers.” 
Wholesale Water Sales Revenue 
Wholesale Rate -Setting Process . Wholesale Customer rates are determined annually based upon the 
Wholesale Customers’ collective share of the Water Enterprise’s total revenue requirements, known as the “Wholesale 
Revenue Requirement” in the WSA. The Wholesale Revenue Requirement under the WSA consists of the sum of 
the Wholesale Customers’ allocated shares of the following costs of the Water Enterprise in providing water to the 
Wholesale Customers: operating and maintenance expenses, capital expenses, administrative and general expenses, 
property taxes, and the “Suburban Hetch Hetchy Assessment,” being the costs of operating the Hetch Hetchy Project 
allocated exclusively to the Water Enterprise or jointly to the Water Enterprise and the Power Enterprise.  Payments 
made by Wholesale Customers can as a general matter be considered by Wholesale Customers to be costs of purchased 
water, an operation and maintenance expense under applicable accounting rules. 
The cost of service for the Wholesale Customers includes a pro- rata share of Operation and Maintenance 
Costs of the Enterprise. Capital costs are recovered under the cash method as needed to cover revenue- funded capital 
improvements of the Regional Water System and debt service associated wi th bond -funded capital projects. The 
operating costs and plant investment for Hetch Hetchy Water and Power are first classified as power -specific, 
water-specific or joint. The water -related costs and the Water Enterprise’s  share of joint costs are reflected in the 
Wholesale Revenue Requirement. 
Finally, the WSA contains a rate device known as the balancing account (the “Balancing Account ”). If the 
amount of the revenues received in any Fiscal Year is greater than the actual expenses associated with the allocated 
cost of wholesale service, the amount of the excess is added to the Balancing Account . If the amount of the revenues 
received in any Fiscal Year is less than the actual expenses associated with the allocated cost of wholesale service, the 88 
amount of the deficit is subtracted from the Balancing Account . As of the end of any Fiscal Year, the Balancing 
Account may have a positive balance (reflecting amounts “o wed” by the SFPUC to the Wholesale Customers) or a 
negative balance (reflecting amounts “owed” by the Wholesale Customers to the SFPUC). The Balancing Account  
balance is used to adjust the following year’s rate recovery up or down depending on whether ther e is a shortfall or 
surplus in the Balancing Account . For Fiscal Year 2022 -23, the Balancing Account  balance was approximately 
$10.1 million due from the Wholesale Customers , and for Fiscal Year 2023- 24, the Balancing Account balance was 
approximately $26.1 million due from the Wholesale Customers  (i.e., such amounts were “owed ” by the Wholesale 
Customers to the SFPUC) . See “APPENDIX B – SUMMARY OF CERTAIN PROVISIONS OF THE WATER 
SUPPLY AGREEMENT – Balancing Account.” 
Statutory and Contractual Limits on Who lesale Water Rates . The sale and delivery of water to the 
Wholesale Customers under the WSA are subject to the “Rules  and Regulations Governing Water Service to 
Customers” of the Water Enterprise adopted by the Commission, as they may from time to time be amended, that are 
(1) applicable to the sale and delivery of water by the SFPUC to the Wholesale Customers, (2) reasonable, and (3) not 
inconsistent with either the WSA or with an individual contract. 
Wholesale Water Rate Adjustments . Under the WSA, adjust ments to the Wholesale Customers’ rate 
schedules, other than emergency rate adjustments and drought pricing, discussed below, are coordinated with the 
budget development process. If the SFPUC intends to increase Wholesale Customer rates, it is required to provide 
certain yearly budget information to the Wholesale Customers prior to adoption of any such rate increases. Failure to 
do so will not prohibit the SFPUC from adoption of such rates, but, in the event of such failure, the Wholesale 
Customers may either invoke arbitration, or seek injunctive relief to compel the SFPUC to remedy the failure as soon 
as reasonably practical. 
The SFPUC may increase the water rates applicable to the Wholesale Customers without compliance with 
the above-described procedures in the event of a drought, earthquake, other act of God, malfunctioning of the Regional 
Water System or other emergency which requires an increase in rates. Rates may be increased on an emergency basis 
to cover operating expenses and capital costs. Any suc h emergency rate increase must be accompanied by a rate 
increase for Retail Customers of an equal percentage. 
Any emergency rate surcharge adopted by the SFPUC could go into effect within 30 days of such adoption 
(subject to any action by the Board of Supervisors) and would remain in effect only until the next budget coordinated 
rate-setting cycle, at which time it can be reviewed for continuance and modification.  
Drought pricing for Wholesale Customers, if required, could also be changed under similar term s and 
conditions set forth for emergency rate increases. Any drought -related pricing or surcharge adopted by the 
Commission would also remain in effect only until the next budget coordinated rate -setting cycle.

Arbitration for Disputes . The Prior Master Water Sales Contract had a binding arbitration provision for 
disputes related to wholesale rate setting by the SFPUC. The SFPUC and its Wholesale Customers arbitrated one 
dispute over the 25- year term of the Prior Master Water Sales Contract. The WSA continues the practice of binding 
arbitration, and the SFPUC and its Wholesale Customers have resolved other disputes, without invoking arbitration, 
as part of the true-up process for determining the actual Wholesale Revenue Requirement following the close of each 
fiscal year. 
Retail Water Sales Revenue 
Retail Rate Structure . Retail Customers pay a flat monthly service charge based on meter size plus a 
volumetric charge for water usage based on one -month meter readings. V olumetric charges for single - and 
multi-family residential customers are based on a two-tiered rate structure, where the first tier is applicable to the first 
4 CCF (400 cubic feet) of use per month (single -family) or 3 CCF (300 cubic feet) of use per month (multi -family), 
and the second tier is applicable to all additional use for both single - and multi- family residential customers. 
V olumetric charges for non-residential customers are based on a uniform rate. The tables below detail retail water rates 
for Fiscal Years 2023-24 and 2024-25.

Retail Rate-Setting Process. The SFPUC is authorized and required under the Charter and Proposition E to 
set rates, fees, and other charges in connection with providing the utility services under its jurisdiction, subject to 
rejection – within 30 days of submission – by resolution of the Board of Supervisors. If the Board of Supervisors fails 
to act within 30  days, the rates will become effective without further action.  If the Board of Supervisors rejects 
proposed rates, the prior rates remain in effect. 
Under the Charter, in setting retail rates, fees, and charges (for water, wastewater and power utility services) 
the SFPUC is required to do the following: 
(1) Establish rates, fees, and charges at levels sufficient to improve or maintain financial condition and 
bond ratings at or above levels equivalent to highly rated utilities of each enterprise under its 
jurisdiction, meet requirements and covenants under all bond resolutions and indentures (including, 
without limitation, increases necessary to pay for the retail customers’ share of the debt service on 
bonds and operating expenses of any State financing authority), and provide sufficient resources for 
the continued financial health (including appropriate reserves), operation, maintenance and repair 
of each enterprise, consistent with good utility practice. 
(2) Retain an independent rate consultant to conduct rate and cost of service studies for each utility at 
least once every five years. 
(3) Set retail rates, fees, and charges based on the cost of service. 
(4) Conduct all studies mandated by applicable State and federal law to consider implementing 
connection fees for water and clean water facilities servicing new development.  
(5) Conduct studies of rate-based conservation incentives and rate structures to help low-income users, 
and to use the results of such studies to establish rates, fees, and charges, in accordance with 
applicable State and federal laws. 
(6) Adopt annually a rolling five-year forecast of rates, fees and other charges. 
(7) Establish a Rate Fairness Board consisting of seven members: the City Administrator or their 
designee; the Controller or their designee; the Director of the Mayor’s Office of Public Finance or 
their designee; two residential r etail customers, one appointed by the Mayor and one by the Board 
of Supervisors; and two business retail customers, one a large business customer appointed by the 
Mayor and one a small business customer appointed by the Board of Supervisors. Duties for the  
Rate Fairness Board include: 
(a) an annual review of a five-year rate forecast; 
(b) hold one or more public hearings on annual rate recommendations before the SFPUC 
adopts rates; 
(c) provide a report and recommendations to the SFPUC on any rate proposal; and,  
(d) in connection with periodic rate studies, recommend rate policies and cost allocations 
among retail utility customer classifications, subject to any outstanding bond requirements. 
The Rate Fairness Board is not authorized to reject proposed rates approved by the Commission. 
Retail rates and the rate -setting process must also comply with the requirements of the State Constitution, 
including notice, protest and public hearing requirements. See “CONSTITUTIONAL, STATUTORY AND 
CHARTER LIMITATIONS – State Law Limitations.” 92 
Retail Water Rate Adjustments. The SFPUC’s retail rates and charges for delivered water are set to equal the 
cost of operation, maintenance, replacement, debt service and other costs incurred in gathering, treating, and delivering 
water to its retail customers. The SFPUC regularly reviews and has often increased its retail water rates to fund 
operating and capital costs. Furthermore, the SFPUC may adjust and re -classify rates, fees, and charges, but will not 
decrease them unless, following such reduction, the rates, fees, and charges will continue to be sufficient to meet the 
rate covenants set forth in the Indenture. See “SECURITY FOR THE BONDS – Rate Covenants.” 
In 2022, pursuant to the Charter, the SFPUC retained an independent rate consultant to conduct a water and 
wastewater cost of service study to establish a proposed three-year water and wastewater retail rate schedule for Fiscal 
Years 2023-24 through 2025-26 (the “2023 Rate Study”). The SFPUC took several measures to mit igate the risk of 
over-projecting volumes, which could potentially lead to revenue shortfalls. First, projections in the 2023 Rate Study 
were based on actual water usage for Fiscal Year 2022-23, which reflected decreased water usage due to the COVID-19 
pandemic, conservation related to the recent drought, and observed population declines in San Francisco. Second, the 
projections in the 2023 Rate Study assumed that San  Francisco never fully recovers from the decline in water usage 
caused by the pandemic and the resulting shift to remote work and declining daytime population. See “THE WATER 
ENTERPRISE – Recent Factors Impacting Retail Water Sales.” Finally, instead of approving rates for four years, as 
was done following the two prior rate studies, the SFPUC approved a three -year rate schedule to allow for a 
realignment of rates to observed sales volumes on a more frequent basis should further declines materialize. Following 
completion of the 2023 Rate Study in April 2023, the Commission approved a three-year water and wastewater retail 
rate schedule for Fiscal Years 2023 -24 through 2025- 26 on May 23, 2023. In accordance with the Charter, the next 
rate study will be conducted by Fiscal Year 2026-27.

Discount Programs . Effective in  2023, the SFPUC’s Customer Assistance Program offers a two- tiered 
discount off water charges for individually metered single -family residential customers. Customers with incomes 
below 50% of the area median income receive a 25% discount and customers with incomes below 30% of the area 
median income receive a 40% discount. In addition, the SFPUC’s Low-Income Non-Profit Housing Program offers a 
15% discount off water charges for residential customers in properties owned and operated by certain nonprofits. 
Finally, the SFPUC’s Community Housing Program offers a 15% discount off water charges for certain single-room-
occupancy hotels providing transitional housing to the homeless and general assistance recipients.  
Billing and Collection Procedures . Generally, all Retail Customers are billed monthly for their respective 
metered water use. In the event of non -payment, the SFPUC has the authority and power to discontinue service and, 
in owner-occupied buildings and master metered apartment buildings, to record liens on property. 
Delayed Billing. The SFPUC relies on Advanced Metering Infrastructure (“AMI”) for the collection of its 
customer usage information for almost all of its customers.  The AMI system uses a transmitter to relay water meter 
readings to the SFPUC billing system.  The AMI system in use is at the mid-point of its useful life and is beginning to 
experience an acceleration of transmitter malfunction with unit components requiring replacement. In summer 2023, 
the SFPUC experienced a procurement delay fo r transmitter replacement parts due to supply chain challenges with 
the vendor, resulting in a growing backlog of unbilled accounts across the SFPUC’s San Francisco service area. In 
winter 2023-24, the SFPUC’s Customer Services Bureau experienced significant turnover of staff, affecting its ability 
to address the backlog of malfunctioning meter transmission units. Though still recording water usage, the billing 
system defaulted to estimated monthly billings for three months when a malfunction was detected, and ceased billing 
if the malfunctioning meter was not addressed. The number of affected unbilled accounts dated back to fall 2023, 
resulting in approximately $6.6 million in unbilled revenues that were accrued as a receivable in Fiscal Year 2023-24. 
The SFPUC immediately implemented an action plan to bill and collect the unbilled revenues that included  
prioritizing and accelerating field service visits to address malfunctioning meter transmission units; r eassigning 
plumbers from other divisions to accelerate the replacement of the malfunctioning AMI components for usage data to 
be transmitted from affected meters ; e xpediting the  hiring of new staff and retain ing contractors to accelerate 
processing of delayed bills for affected accounts ; enhancing the billing system to allow for extended bill estimates , 
increasing the threshold from three months to 36 months ; and developing an automated workflow to expedite the 
processing of affected accounts with delayed bills and payment plans. 
As part of the action plan, the SFPUC also initiated an extensive and proactive customer outreach campaign 
to provide advance notice to all affected customers of their delayed billing and when it would resume. To assist 
customers unable to pay the entire amount due, the SFPUC is offering payment plans of up to nine months. The 
SFPUC anticipates collecting the majority of delayed revenues by June 30, 2025.  
Delinquencies. The SFPUC reviews water accounts routinely to determine whether they have a past due 
balance that is delinquent. Typically, once an account is delinquent, a series of notices are sent to the customer and if 
payment is still not made, action to shut-off the water supply is initiated. However, if the delinquent account is in the 
name of the property owner and payment is still not made despite such notices, the customer will receive a lien warning 
notice. After a lien notice is delivered, a lien hearing is held and, if the bill still remains outstanding, the lien is recorded 
and can only be removed upon full payment of all unpaid charges, plus administrative fees and interest. Liens not paid 94 
during the Fiscal Year in which they are recorded are transferred to the City Tax Collector’s Office, Bureau of 
Delinquent Revenues, for collection as a lien against the property. Accounts for which property transfers occurred 
prior to recording the lien and closed accounts with amounts less than $25 are normally written off as uncollectible.  
The number of water accounts that are delinquent and the amount of accounts receivable from delinquent 
bills have grown significantly since the beginning of the COVID -19 pandemic. As of July 1, 2019, the number of 
delinquent water accounts more than 90 da ys past due was 3,968 and the amount of accounts receivable from such 
delinquent water accounts totaled approximately $520.1 thousand. By contrast, as of July 1, 2024, the number of 
delinquent water accounts more than 90 days past due was 22,433 and the amount of accounts receivable from such 
delinquent water accounts totaled approximately $10.5 million. The table below shows the amount of accounts 
receivable from delinquent water accounts for Retail Customers as of July 1, 2022, July 1, 2023 and July 1, 2024.

In March 2020, in response to the COVID -19 pandemic, the SFPUC first suspended shutoffs of water and 
sewer service and prohibited the imposition of late payment penalties, fees, and charges (the “COVID- 19 
Moratorium”). In addition, in May 2020, the SFPUC first authorized a temporary Emergency Customer Assistance 
Program (“E-CAP”), offering a discount off water charges for qualifying residential and commercial ratepayers. 
Between 2020 and 2023, the SFPUC periodically extended and modified the COVID -19 Moratorium and E-CAP. In 
2023, the SFPUC changed the Customer Assistance Program to incorporate practices from E -CAP (for current 
program details, see “FINANCIAL OPERATIONS – Retail Water Sales Revenue – Discount Programs”), and further 
approved exemptions from disconnections of water and sewer service and liens due to unpaid bills for households 
enrolled in the Customer Assistance Program and for customers who fall between 51% to 80% of area median income 
who experience extenuating circumstances. In November 2024, the Commission approved sunsetting the COVID -19 
pandemic procedures for water and sewer customer bill collection and delinquency actions; resuming normal business 
practices for shutoffs, liens, and fines; amending water and wastewater shutoff and lien policies to provide exemptions, 
subject to limitations, to (i) customers with an outstanding bill balance of $100 or less, (ii) customers enrolled in the 
Customer Assistance Program, and (iii) customers within defined area mean income limits who qualify for extenuating 
circumstances. Such changes went into effect January 1, 2025. The SFPUC anticipates that utilizing all collection 
tools available will result in a significant reduction of delinquent water accounts.  95

Affordability. Nearly all Retail Customers are also served by the Wastewater Enterprise and such Retail 
Customers receive a single monthly bill reflecting both charges for water service and charges for sewer service. The 
SFPUC’s affordability efforts are therefore focused on the cost of water service and sewer service viewed as a whole.  
The SFPUC is engaged in a major capital program for the Wastewater Enterprise and anticipates that significant 
increases in sewer charges will be required in addition to the anticipated retail rate increases for water service described 
herein. The average residential combined water and sewer bill is projected to increase 8%, 9.7%, and 11.5%, in Fiscal 
Year 2024 -25, Fiscal Year 2025 -26, and Fiscal Year 2026 -27, respectively. Affordability concerns may impact 
discussions with respect to retail rates for water service and may, among other things, require the SFPUC to prioritize 
and defer capital projects for the Water Enterprise. 
Capacity Charges 
The SFPUC imposes a capacity charge on any Retail Customer requesting a new connection, added collection 
or treatment capacity, or a change to an existing connection to the water distribution system. Beginning July 1, 2024, 
the capacity charge is $2,266 per equivalent 5/8-inch meter. The capacity charge is adjusted on July 1 of each year by 
the annual change in the 20-City Average Construction Cost Index published by ENR Magazine. 
Operating and Maintenance Expenses 
“Operating and Maintenance Expenses ” cover the general operations expenses of the Water Enterprise. 
These expenses include labor and fringe benefits, contractual services, materials and supplies, depreciation, general 
and administrative, services from other departments and other miscellaneous costs. See “HISTORICAL OPERATING 
RESULTS.” Services from other departments include payment for services from other City departments, such as the 
City Attorney’s Office and the General Services Agency. Operating and Maintenance Expenses include payments to 
Hetch Hetchy Water and Power for services related to water storage and delivery. See “– Inter-Enterprise Transfers” 
and “SECURITY FOR THE BONDS – Rate Covenants.” 
Allocation of Costs. Various common costs incurred by the SFPUC are allocated among the Water Enterprise, 
the Wastewater Enterprise and the Power Enterprise. Allocations are based on methodology established by an 
independent indirect cost study, and SFPUC management’s best estimates and may change from year to year 
depending on activities undertaken by each enterprise and information available. The SFPUC allocated $66.1 million 
in administrative costs to the Water Enterprise for Fiscal Year 2023-24, which are included in the financial statements 
under various expense categories, and has budgeted $65.0 million and $67.3 million for such costs for Fiscal Years 
2024-25 and 2025-26, respectively. 96 
Inter-Enterprise Transfers. An annual transfer occurs from the Water Enterprise to Hetch Hetchy Water and 
Power to pay for services related to water storage and delivery. The annual transfer amount was $46.3 million for 
Fiscal Year 2023-24, and the SFPUC has budgeted $49.2 million and $52.2 million for such transfers for Fiscal Years 
2024-25 and 2025- 26, respectively.  Should Hetch Hetchy Water and Power incur higher capital costs or higher 
operating costs in the future, the amount of these transfers could increase. 
Payments to/from the City. 
Payments to City for Interdepartmental Services. A variety of City departments provide services 
such as engineering, purchasing, legal, telecommunications and human resources to the Water Enterprise, 
and charge amounts designed to recover those costs. These charges totaled approximately $27.6 million for 
Fiscal Year 2023-24, and the SFPUC has budgeted $32.0 million and $34.1 million for such charges for Fiscal 
Years 2024-25 and 2025-26, respectively. 
Lease Certificate of Participation Financing. On October 7, 2009, the City issued $167.67 million 
in fixed-rate Certificates of Participation, Series 2009 C and D, to fund the headquarters of the SFPUC at 
525 Golden Gate Avenue. Pursuant to a Memorandum of Understanding between the City and the SFPUC, 
the SFPUC agreed to reimburse the City General Fund for all costs in connection with this City financing. 
This obligation is subordinate to debt service on the Bonds and payments related thereto are allocated among 
the three SFPUC Enterprises. See “OBLIGATIONS PAYABLE FROM REVENUES – Other Subordinate 
Obligations Payable from Revenues.” 
Water Payments from Other Agencies. The SFPUC receives payments fr om other agencies of the 
City for their share of the proportionate cost of the service provided to them. The Water Enterprise charges 
all City departments for water (with the exception of itself and the San  Francisco Fire Department for water 
dispensed from fire hydrants). The SFPUC collected approximately $12.5 million in payments for Fiscal Year 
2023-24, and has budgeted to collect $11.9 million and $12.8 million in payments for Fiscal Years 2024- 25 
and 2025-26, respectively. 
Financial Management Policies 
The SFPUC makes no representation that the following policies and targets will not be revised or amended 
and, except to the extent required for compliance with the terms of the Indenture, makes no representation that these 
policies will be followed by the SFPUC or that targets will be met. 
Debt Management Policies and Procedures . The SFPUC has established “Debt Management Policies and 
Procedures” (the “Debt Policies”) for debt financing under its jurisdiction. The SFPUC has also established separate 
“SFPUC Municipal Securities Disclosure Policies and Procedures” (the “Disclosure Policies ”) which are appended 
to the Debt Policies. The Debt Policies, including the appended Disclosure Policies, apply to all SFPUC enterprises, 
including the Water Enterprise, an d are intended to enable the SFPUC to effectively manage its debt issuance and 
administration practices and comply with all debt issuance and administration rules and regulations. The Debt Policies 
are reviewed bi-annually and revised, as necessary, with Commission approval. 
Revisions to the Debt Policies were most recently adopted by the Commission in 2019. This included 
revisions to three key areas: 
(1) Disclosure – The Disclosure Policies were revised to cover two amendments that were made to Rule 
15c2-12, effective February  27, 2019 (the “ Rule 15c2 -12 Amendments”). See the Listed Events 15 and 16 in 
“APPENDIX E – FORM OF CONTINUING DISCLOSURE CERTIFICATE.” In addition, pursuant to the Disclosure 
Policies, the SFPUC developed a Disclosure Practices Workin g Group (the “ DPWG”). The DPWG meets at least 
semiannually to better and more effectively administer the SFPUC’s continuing disclosure requirements, particularly 
to monitor the SFPUC’s financial obligations and financial difficulties, if any, in light of the Rule 15c2 -12 
Amendments. The DPWG is comprised of the Chief Financial Officer and Assistant General Manager, Business 
Services, two Deputy Chief Financial Officers and the Debt Manager of the SFPUC, with legal consultation from the 
City Attorney’s Office and the SFPUC’s outside disclosure counsel. 97 
(2) Variable Rate Obligations – The Debt Policies, which set forth and describe various types of debt 
obligations that the SFPUC may issue, were revised to cover new amendments made to the Wastewater bond indenture 
that will allow the SFPUC to issue variable rate obligations of the Wastewater Enterprise with more market- standard 
provisions. These amendments do not apply to any variable rate obligations issued by the Water Enterprise. 
(3) WIFIA Loans – The Debt Policies were revised to add WIFIA loans as a permitted form of low-cost 
debt financing instrument, comparable to the low-cost State Revolving Fund loans that the SFPUC has, from time-to-
time, executed with the SWRCB. No WIFIA loans have been executed for the Water Enterprise. 
Debt Service Coverage Policy. The Commission adopted a debt service coverage policy (the “Debt Service 
Coverage Policy”) in 2017, which applies to all SFPUC enterprises, including the Water Enterprise. Pursuant to the 
Debt Service Coverage Policy, to ensure that the SFPUC maintains access to low -cost capital and retains financial 
flexibility for contingencies, the SFPUC will aim to adopt budgets, rates and financial plans that generate revenues 
such that debt service coverage calculated on an Indenture basis (including certain available fund balances) will be at 
least 1.35 times and debt service coverage calculated on a current basis (Net Revenues divided by debt service on 
Bonds and Parity Loans) will be at least 1.10 times. 
Separate from the Debt Service Coverage Policy, the Indenture includes a rate covenant that debt service 
coverage calculated on an Indenture basis (including certain available fund balances) will be at least 1.25 times. See 
“SECURITY FOR THE BONDS – Rate Covenants – Debt Service Coverage” and “HISTORICAL OPERATING 
RESULTS.” 
Capital Financing Policy . The Commission adopted a capital financing policy (the “ Capital Financing 
Policy”) in 2017, which applies to all SFPUC enterprises, including the Water Enterprise. The SFP UC relies mainly 
on current revenue and debt financing to pay for capital assets or improvements. According to the Capital Financing 
Policy, the appropriate mix of current revenues versus debt financing depends, in part, on the capital investment 
lifecycle of the Water Enterprise. Accordingly, the SFPUC has determined that over the 10- year financial planning 
horizon, the SFPUC will aim to pay for a minimum ranging between 15% and 30% of the Water Enterprise’s capital 
budget from current revenues. 
Fund Balance Reserve Policy. The Commission adopted a fund balance reserve policy (the “Fund Balance 
Reserve Policy”) in 2017, which applies to all SFPUC enterprises, including the Water Enterprise. Pursuant to the 
Fund Balance Reserve Policy, for the time period covered in the 10-year financial plan, the SFPUC will aim to propose 
operating and capital budgets and rates for adoption such that the Fund Balance Reserve totals a minimum of 90 days 
or 25% of operations and maintenance expenses (including programmatic projects and excluding debt service and 
revenue-funded capital) throughout the forecast period. Amounts in excess of such minimum will be considered for 
contingencies and rate stabilization. 
Affordability Policy . The Commission adopted its most recent af fordability policy (the “ Affordability 
Policy”) in 2023, which applies to the Water Enterprise and the Wastewater Enterprise. Pursuant to the Affordability 
Policy, the SFPUC will, when adopting rates and approving budgets and financial plans, review the average residential 
combined water and sewer bill over the next 20 years, and aim to set rates that will result in the average residential 
combined bill not exceeding 3% of income for a “typical” customer (defined as the 40th percentile San Francisco 
household income), 7% of income for a “low -income” customer (defined as the 20th percentile San Francisco 
household income), and 5% of income for a “low -income” customer after accounting for enrollment in applicable 
low-income discount programs. If such average r esidential combined bills are projected to exceed the targets within 
the 20-year period, the SFPUC must provide a rationale and develop strategies to reduce costs, including providing 
progress reports to the Commission in subsequent budget and financial plan adoptions.  
Investment of SFPUC Funds 
The SFPUC’s pooled deposits and investments are invested pursuant to State law and the investment policy 
established from time to time by the City Treasurer and overseen by the Treasury Oversight Committee. The curre nt 
policy seeks the preservation of capital, liquidity and yield, in that order of priority. Under the City Treasurer’s current 
investment procedures, the SFPUC’s pooled deposits and investments are invested in the City’s larger pooled 
investment fund (the  “City Pool”). Among other purposes, the City Pool serves in effect as a disbursement account 98 
for expenditures from the City’s various segregated and pooled funds. Investments are generally made so that securities 
can be held to maturity. The City Treasure r calculated the weighted average maturity of these investments as of 
November 30, 2024, to be 516 days (most recent data available). 
The following table sets forth the approximate book values of the investments held in the City Pool reported 
by the City Treasurer as of  November 30, 2024. The Water Enterprise’s pooled deposits and investments accounted 
for approximately $390.4 million, or approximately 2.38% of such amounts. The Wastewater Enterprise and the Power 
Enterprise each have their own pooled deposits and investments that are separate from the Water Enterprise.
