  114 
REGULATORY MATTERS 
General 
Public water supply systems in California, such as the Regional Water System and In -City Distribution 
System operated by the Water Enterprise, are primarily regulated by the SWRCB Division of Drinking Water 
(“DDW”), formerly under the California Department of Public Health (“ CDPH”) and, in some limited instances, by 
the USEPA and California Regional Water Quality Control Boards (“RWQCBs”). 
Drinking water delivered to customers must comply with statutory and regulatory water quality standards 
designed to protect public health that are now administered by DDW. The CDPH reissued a drinking water supply 
permit in 2004 prescribing conditions and requirements for the Water Enterprise to specifically operate the Regional 
Water System. The CDPH also issued drinking water supply permits to the In-City Distribution System, several small 
water systems owned and operated by the Water Enterprise, and the wholesale customer public water supply systems. 
The CDPH and the DDW issued several amendments to the Regional Water System and In -City Distribution System 
permits for various changes over the last 20 years. In accordance with the drinking water standards and permit 
requirements, the Water Enterprise operates and maintains water storage, treatment and conveyance facilities, 
implements watershed management and protection activities, performs inspections, monitors drinking water quality , 
conducts applied research, maintains and implements a comprehensive and effective cross -connection control 
program, and submits monthly and annual compliance reports. The Water Enterprise is currently operating in 
compliance with all State and federal drinking water regulations and permit requirements. The drinking water supply 
permits for both Regional Water System and In-City Distribution System are scheduled to be updated around 2030 to 
reflect new facilities and operations. 
In addition, public water system discharges to State and federal waters are regulated under general National 
Pollutant Discharge Elimination System (“ NPDES”) permits. The SWRCB issue s general permits, whereas the two 
RWQCBs (San Francisco Bay and Central Valley) issue individ ual region-specific NPDES permits to the SFPUC, 
which contain numerical effluent limitations, monitoring, reporting and notification requirements for water discharges 
from the facilities and pipelines of the Regional Water System. The SFPUC is generally op erating and maintaining 
the water treatment and transmission facilities in compliance with the NPDES permit requirements. Coverage under 
existing NPDES permits will remain since the permits issued by the SWRCB or RWQCBs are either reissued on a 
specific timeline or administratively extended. The SFPUC will ensure continuous coverage and re -enroll under 
NPDES permits as necessary. 
A number of water resource management and regulatory initiatives may affect the availability of water to the 
Regional Water Syste m in the future. Also, alternate water supplies currently used by wholesale customers of the 
Water Enterprise may be reduced in the future, increasing the customers’ reliance on the Regional Water System. In 
addition to those raised below, these initiative s include the Sacramento -San Joaquin Delta Reform Act of 2009, the 
federal or California Endangered Species Acts, the SWRCB Bay- Delta Water Quality Control Plan a mendment 
proceedings, the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (“ FERC”) proceeding to relicense the Irrigation Districts’ 
Don Pedro Hydropower Project sited on its New Don Pedro Dam and Reservoir and others. The effects of any of these 
activities, or of these activities cumulatively, are unknown. 
Drinking Water Requirements 
Regulatory Agency.  The USEPA and the DDW have picked up their pace updating existing and adopting 
new drinking water regulations. These new developments will impact the current operations of the SFPUC’s drinking 
water systems as new resources may be needed to comply with these new and/or updated regulatory requirements.  
Recent Adoption of Drinking Water Regulations .  The DDW  has adopted many new drinking water 
regulations at the State level in the last five years. These include a primary maximum contaminant level (“MCL”) for 
hexavalent chromium; direct potable reuse regulations; cross-connection control policy; a much lower detection limit, 
from 6 to 1 microgram per liter (“µg/L”), for reporting of perchlorate level in drinking water; the Total Coliform Rule 
Revision; a policy handbook for monitoring of microplastics in drinking water; and notification and response level for 
per- and poly-fluorinated alkyl substances (“ PFAS”). In the last five years, the USEPA adopted two drinking water 
regulations consisting of MCLs and Hazard Index for six PFAS contaminants (including PFOS, PFOA, PFBS, PFHxS 115 
and two others), the Lead and Copper Rule Revision (“ LCRR”) and the Lead and Copper Rule Improvements 
(“LCRI”), and Consumer Confidence Report Revision regulations. The USEPA also anti cipates proposing revisions 
to microbial and disinfection byproduct rules in summer 2025.  
Near-Term Regulatory Development.  Several regulatory developments at the State level are anticipated in 
the near future, including but not limited to the following: revision of the existing Lead and Copper Rule (“LCR”) in 
line with the corresponding federal LCRR and LCRI; increase in publication frequency of a Consumer Confidence 
Report from annual to semiannual to conform to the recently adopted federal Consumer Confidence Report Revision 
regulations; adoption of new MCLs for PFOS and PFAS; adoption of n otification levels and response levels related 
to harmful algal bloom, PFAS, and manganese, and lowering detection limits for reporting purposes for eight metals 
including lead, nickel and arsenic. At the federal level, the USEPA has committed to issuing a drinking water regulation 
for setting perchlorate MCL by May 2027, and is in the process of developing the stage 3 microbial/disinfection 
byproduct regulation.  
Regulatory Review by USEPA and DDW. Under the Safe Drinking Water Act (“SDWA”), the USEPA must 
review all existing regulations every six years and determine which regulations may need to be revised. The fourth 
Six-Year Review of existing National Pr imary Drinking Water Regulations was completed in July 2024, and the 
USEPA determined that there are no additional candidates identified for regulatory revision.  However, the Six- Year 
Review is not a final regulatory decision to revise or not revise a drinking water regulation; rather, it is part of a 
pre-planning process to determine if a rulemaking effort to revise the regulation should be considered.  
Similarly, California Health and Safety Code §116365(g) requires the DDW to review its MCLs at least once 
every five years. These quinquennial MCL reviews are to ensure that the drinking water standards cannot be less 
stringent than the corresponding federal values and must be as close as is technically and economically feasible to the 
public health goals established by the California Environmental Protection Agency’s Office of Environmental Health 
and Hazard Assessment (“OEHHA”). The latest review was conducted between 2015 and 2017 and concluded by the 
SWRCB in 2018 that no MCL revisions were needed.  Nevertheless, regulatory changes resulting in the need for new 
infrastructure and operations may incur in future MCL reviews by USEPA and DDW with respect to the following 
existing drinking water regulations: 
Surface Water Treatment. Several related regulations  addressing surface water treatment requirements for 
drinking water were adopted by the USEPA and the DDW between 1990 and 2010. These regulations, including the 
Surface Water Treatment Rule, Interim Enhanced Surface Water Treatment, Filter Backwash Recycl ing Rule, 
Long-Term 1 and Long- Term 2 Enhanced Surface Water Treatment Rules, require filtration of all surface water 
supplies unless the water supply can meet certain stringent requirements.  These regulations also impose microbial 
disinfection/inactivation performance with which community water systems including those operated by the SFPUC 
have to comply. 
As discussed under “WATER FACILITIES – Water Treatment,” the high quality of water stored at Hetch 
Hetchy Reservoir has been determined to be sufficient  to meet filtration avoidance requirements without installation 
and operation of filtration facilities. However, new treatment (disinfection or filtration) facilities could be required in 
the future if the filtration avoidance criteria are not consistently  met or due to changes in these regulations. See 
“– Regulatory Review by USEP A and DDW.” 
Local water from the Alameda and Peninsula watersheds and upcountry non- Hetch Hetchy sources requires 
filtration to meet applicable drinking water quality requirements. The filtered and treated water from these non-Hetch 
Hetchy sources are typically blended with the disinfected Hetch Hetchy water. Most customers receive water from 
blended sources. The SFPUC’s Tesla Treatment Facility uses ultraviolet light technology to inactivate target organisms 
(Giardia and Cryptosporidium) in the Hetch Hetchy water supply; it has been performing well since its operations 
began in 2012. See “WATER FACILITIES – Water Treatment.”  
System water quality, including both raw water and treat ed water, is continuously monitored and tested 
year-round to assure that water delivered to customers meets or exceeds federal and State drinking water/public health 
requirements. Nevertheless, more rigorous water quality monitoring efforts may be required  as a result of new or 
updated drinking water regulations that are adopted by USEPA and/or DDW. 116 
Stage 2 Disinfectants and Disinfection Byproduct Rule. The USEPA promulgated the Stage 2 Disinfectants 
and Disinfection Byproducts Rule (“ Stage 2 DBPR ”) in 2006 and the State adopted its version of the same rule in 
2012. This rule is to reduce public health risk associated with disinfection byproducts (“ DBPs”) in drinking water. 
Since then, the SFPUC has demonstrated compliance with the Stage 2 DBPR by the continued use of the 
chloramination treatment process.  
Under the SDWA Six-Year Review Rule, USEPA decided to review existing disinfectant and disinfection 
byproduct rules. The USEPA anticipates proposing revisions to microbial and disinfection byproduct rules in summer 
2025. 
Groundwater Rule. The USEPA promulgated the Groundwater Rule (“GWR”) to address waterborne disease 
and microbial contamination related to groundwater. The DDW adopted its own version of the GWR in 2011 by 
reference to the USEPA’s regulations. The GWR requires that a system putting new groundwater sources in service 
must conduct assessment source -water monitoring if directed by the State. The GWR also requires a groundwater 
system to conduct triggered source -water monitoring if it d oes not provide 4- log virus treatment and the results of 
bacteriological monitoring are fecal -coliform positive. The SFPUC presently has a groundwater program consisting 
of two projects: San  Francisco Groundwater Supply for the In -City Distribution, and Gr oundwater Storage and 
Recovery for the Regional Water System. The groundwater wells of these two projects will serve as the additional and 
emergency water supplies to the In-City Distribution System and the Regional Water System. The requirements of this 
rule are applicable to these two groundwater systems, and the extent of impacts depends on the operational 
management and water quality of these new alternate groundwater sources. Additional treatment facilities may be 
required to meet the disinfection requirements and to reduce certain mineral content of the groundwater to comply 
with the corresponding drinking water standards, if water quality monitoring results indicated such a need and the 
approved blending treatment with the surface water sources is insufficient to meet the existing and/or future drinking 
water standards. 
Revised Total Coliform Rule. The USEPA adopted the Revised Total Coliform Rule (“RTCR”) in 2013 and 
the DDW adopted its own version of the RTCR that has been effective since July 1, 2021.  Both the Regional Water 
System and the In- City Distribution System are in compliance with these regulatory requirements. The USEPA is in 
the process of developing a new rule regulating the operations of distribution systems and storage facilities of al l 
community water system in the nearest future. It is too early at this time to identify what new treatment facilities or 
operational changes will be required to meet this future rule. 
Lead and Copper Rule. The SFPUC proactively addressed the issue of lead in water well before the USEPA 
published its Lead and Copper Rule in 1991 and the State’s adoption of similar regulations in the 1990s. The federal 
LCR was subsequently updated with several regulatory actions that resulted in the promulgation of 2000 LCR Minor 
Revisions, 2007 LCR Short-Term Revisions, 2021 LCRR, and 2024 LCRI. 
In the 1980s, the SFPUC removed all known lead service lines and fittings  from the In -City Distribution 
System. In the late 1990s, the SFPUC started distributing non -lead faucets to daycare centers and schools. The 
distribution program for non-lead faucets was then expanded to the general public served by the In- City Distribution 
System. In the 2000s, the SFPUC initiated a program to replace service meters with a non- leaded type in the In-City 
Distribution System and eliminated large, leaded, compound meters. At the present time, approximately 99% of the 
service meters have been replaced. 
In 2006, the SFPUC submitted a report to the State documenting that its existing corrosion control treatment 
using pH adjustment was optimized. The SFPUC also piloted use of other non- leaded plumbing components and 
sponsored the lead-free law known as Assembly Bill 1953 (“AB 1953”) that was enacted by the California Legislature 
in 2006. AB 1953, which requires new lead-free plumbing components containing no more than 0.25% lead, has been 
in effect since January  1, 2010. In January 2014, the USEPA began enforcing its lead -free mandate known as the 
Reduction of Lead in Drinking Water Act, which was enacted by Congress on January 4, 2011. The USEPA’s definition 
of “lead free” is the same as that in AB 1953. 
 In 2024, the SFPUC completed its last triennial LCR monitoring for the In -City Distribution and the 
Regional Water System in accordance with the 1991 LCR. The monitoring results demonstrate continued compliance 
with the existing LCR. Nevertheless, the SFPUC may have to conduct a new optimized corrosion control treatment 117 
study if, in the future, the SFPUC decides to blend new groundwater sources into the Regional Water System and the 
In-City Distribution System.  The next round of LCR monitoring must be performed in accordance with the new LCRI 
requirements. Two rounds of full monitoring must be completed in 2028 before reduced triennial monitoring can be 
requested with the DDW and USEPA. If lead levels exceed the new lower threshold, the SFPUC must notify the public 
and may need to modify its corrosion control treatment. 
In 2016, Section 116885 of the California Health and Safety Code was amended by Senate Bill 1398 and 
Senate Bill 427 requiring a community water system, such as those overseen by the SFPUC, to complete the following: 
(i) compile an inventory of known Lead User Service Lines (“ LUSLs”), which are utility -owned service lines that 
have components made of lead material, by July  1, 2018; (ii) identify areas that may have LUSLs, including service 
lines made of unknown materials, by July 1, 2018; (iii) identify unknown user service line material by July 1, 2020; 
and (iv) develop a replacement timeline for LUSLs and still unknown material user service lines by July 1, 2020. The 
approved SFPUC replacement timeline is 10 years. The 2026 Capital Plan includes the As-Needed Water Service Line 
Replacement Project (also known as the Lead Component Services Program), estimated to cost $38.5 million, and 
funding to complete this work. The SFPUC has also applied for an additional State Revolving Fund loan to finance 
the project. See “OBLIGATIONS PAYABLE FROM REVENUES – Parity State Loans.” This project is 73% complete 
and expected to be completed by June 2026, years ahead of the compliance deadline.  
In January 2021, the LCRR went into effect. As part of the Biden administration’s review of all new 
regulations, the USEPA reviewed the LCRR and decided in 2021 to keep the lead service line inventory requirements 
of the LCRR in place to ensure the initial customer water service line material inventory was completed by October 
2024. The LCRI revised several facets of the LCRR, including lowering the 90th percentile le ad action level from 
15 μg/L to 10 μg/L, revising tap sampling procedures, deferring free lead sampling at schools and licensed childcare 
centers to 2027, and imposing a 10 -year deadline for water systems to conduct replacements for all lead -containing 
service lines within the SFPUC’s control. The DDW is also in the process of updating its own version of the LCR so 
that its regulatory requirements will be at least consistent with the LCRR. The SFPUC has performed inspections and 
used statistical methods to prepare the initial lead service line inventory, identifying 653 customer -owned galvanized 
service lines that were previously downstream of a utility-owned lead service line, and 407 service lines with unknown 
lead status. No lead service lines were found.  The SFPUC expects to be able to identify the material composition of 
service lines with unknown lead status before 2027; otherwise, these customer -owned services lines will need to be 
replaced within a 10-year period. The replacement cost is currently unknown. 
PFAS.  The USEPA adopted primary MCLs for six PFAS on April 10, 2024.  On April 5, 2024, the OEHHA 
adopted public health goals for PFOA and PFOS at 0.007 ng/L and 1 ng/L, respectively, for California. The State law 
requires a MCL established by the DDW to be as close to its public health goals as is technologically and economically 
feasible.     
 The In-City Distribution System and the Regional Water System also completed four rounds of quarterly 
sampling of 29 PFAS components and lithium in early 2024.  This monitoring was required under USEPA’s fifth 
Unregulated Contaminant Monitoring Rule (“UCMR5”). The sampling results gathered throughout the United States 
under UCMR5 will be used by US EPA in its f uture determination of rulemaking needs regarding these 30 
contaminants.   The SFPUC’s PFAS and lithium monitoring results confirmed no detection of these 30 contaminants 
in the drinking water.  The only SFPUC location with detection below regulatory limits has been the small Pleasanton 
Well Field Water System, which is a standalone water system independent of the Regional Water System and the 
In-City Distribution System.  
The City Attorney’s Office has repeatedly opted out of settlement agreements to preserve its rights to seek 
better compensation if an issue related to PFAS contamination in the SFPUC water sources were to develop in the 
future. In addition, on March 3, 2025, the San Francisco City Attorney filed a lawsuit on behalf of the People of the 
State of California in San Francisco Superior Court against certain PFAS manufacturers seeking to recover abatement 
and damages for contamination of San Francisco’s resources. The lawsuit alleges that at least two drinking water wells 
operated by the SFPUC as part of the Pleasanton Well Field Water System have detectable levels of PFAS. The lawsuit 
also alleges PFAS contamination in the City’s other resources, including the City’s combined sewer collection system. 
While the City believes that the claims in this lawsuit are meritorious, it can give no assurance regarding whether the 
lawsuit will be successful and obtain the requested relief from the courts, or contributions to the abatement fund from 
the defendant PFAS manufacturers. 118 
Fluoridation. The 1995 State Assembly Bill 733 requires large water systems to fluoridate their public water 
supply and directs the appropriate State agency to seek funding for fluoridation. The State adopted its fluoridation 
regulation in 1998. The requirements of this regulation, a s codified in Section  64433 through 64434 of Title  22, 
California Code of Regulations, apply to large water systems with at least 10,000 service connections. 
The SFPUC has been fluoridating its water supply since the early 1950s, and meets all the requirem ents of 
fluoridation regulation. In 2015, the United States Department of Health and Human Services Agency recommended 
that water systems practicing fluoridation adjust their fluoride content to 0.7 mg/L, as opposed to the previous 
temperature-dependent optimal levels ranging from 0.7 mg/L to 1.2 mg/L. The Center s for Disease Control and 
Prevention (the “ CDC”) also provided detailed information on the basis for this change. To reflect the CDC’s 
recommendation, the DDW consulted with public water systems practicing fluoridation regarding amendments to their 
individual public water supply permits to reference the CDC’s recommended optimal level of 0.7 mg/L. The DDW 
confirmed in 2016 that the fluoride control range for the Regional Water System is 0.6 mg/L to 1.2 mg/L in addition 
to the recommended optimal level. The SFPUC is in compliance with the prescribed levels and the operational and 
monitoring requirements of the State fluoridation regulation. 
Cross-Connection Control. On December 19, 2023, the DDW adopted a policy handbook that replaces the 
regulations in Title 17 of California Code of Regulations for cross -connection control. Effective starting on July 1, 
2024, the policy handbook establishes enforceable standards and provides guiding principles to California public water 
systems for public health protection through backflow prevention and control of cross-connection.  On December 10, 
2024, the SFPUC amended Section G, Cross -Connection Control, of its Rules and Regulations Governing Water 
Service to Custo mers, to be consistent with the handbook’s new standards. While the handbook has established 
requirements with which the SFPUC is in compliance through its cross -connection control program, its financial 
impacts to the Water Enterprise’s operations are uncertain since these requirements were recently put into effect.  
Ongoing review of the program implementation will need to be conducted. The SFPUC will submit a Cross 
Connection Control Plan to DDW by July 1, 2025, as required by the handbook. 
Direct Potable Reuse. On August 6, 2024, the Office of Administrative Law approved the DDW’s regulations 
on direct potable reuse, which establishes enforceable requirements of water recycling through raw water 
augmentation for drinking water supply.  The SFPUC will not develop new direct potable reuse supplies for some 
time. Operational costs and impacts will be identified during project design.  
Hexavalent Chromium MCL. The DDW adopted an updated hexavalent chromium MCL of 10 µg/L with a 
much lower detection limit for reporting purposes at 0.1 µg/L. The new MCL regulation was approved by Office of 
Administrative Law in July 2024 and went into effect on October 1, 2024.  With the State’s new reporting limit, there 
were occasions in 2024 when the treated water from t he two local treatment plants had low detections of this 
contaminant. While treatment of the SFPUC’s surface water is not anticipated as the detections are far below the MCL, 
the groundwater sources, which have substantially higher hexavalent chromium leve ls, will continue to be treated 
using the DDW-approved blending method to meet the MCL. 
Regulations/Other Contaminants.  The DDW has also initiated the rulemaking process for the following 
MCLs on its priority agenda: N-nitroso-dimethylamine (“ NDMA”), perfluorooctanoic acid (“PFOA ”), and 
perfluorooctane sulfonic acid. (“PFOS”), as well as the review process for possible revisions to a suite of contaminants 
that have existing primary MCLs.  Among these contaminants, arse nic is a primary candidate to be reviewed.  DDW 
is expected to continue increasing its efforts with the ultimate goal of establishing applicable drinking water standards, 
either new or more stringent, to enhance public health protection. These DDW regulations may have unknown impacts 
to the SFPUC’s water operations, dependent on the values of these future MCLs and the improvements in water quality 
analytical technology. 
The DDW is also considering the following: acute and short -term notification and respons e levels for four 
cyanotoxins (microcystins, cylindrospermopsin, anatoxin-α, and saxitoxin) recommended by the OEHHA; and lower 
notification and response levels for manganese at 20 µg/L and 200 µg/L, respectively (existing levels are 500 µg/L 
and 5,000 µg/L, respectively). 
Environmental Laboratory Accreditation Program Regulations . In 2020, the SWRCB adopted new 
Environmental Laboratory Accreditation Program (“ ELAP”) regulations, which are applicable to five laboratories  119 
within the Water Enterprise’s Water Quality Division. While most regulations took effect January 1, 2021, full 
compliance was required by January 1, 2024. All ELAP-accredited laboratories have successfully completed third -
party assessments and demonstrated compliance with the new regulations, which incorporate TNI Standards based on 
ISO 17025. The SFPUC is currently designing a new laboratory facility in Millbrae to replace the existing 50 -year-
old facility. The SFPUC continues to maintain and operate its laboratories in accordance with all regulatory standards 
and plans for future laboratory improvements as needed. 
Public Water System Discharges 
As part of routine operations and maintenance activities, the SFPUC transfers treated water between storage 
facilities and discharges water to the environment. These transfers and discharges are regulated under the federal Clean 
Water Act through general NPDES permits issued by the SWRCB or RWQCB, as appropriate. 
The SFPUC currently has several NPDES permits that cover discharges from the Regional Water System and 
SFPUC drinking water facilities. These permits generally impose discharge limitations, monitoring, reporting, and 
notification requirements. These permits require the SFPUC to control various water quality parameters (such as pH, 
chlorine residual, turbidity, etc.) and implement best management practices to minimize any adverse environmental 
effects caused by the discharges from the Regional Water System.  
On March 4, 2025, the City prevailed in its action against the USEPA regarding an NPDES discharge permit 
issued under the Clean Water Act for the Wastewater Enterprise’s Oceanside Water Pollution Control Plant. The 
dispute centered on “end- result” provisions – permit terms that made the City responsible for water quality in the 
Pacific Ocean – well beyond the quality of the City’s discharges. In City and County of San Francisco v. Environmental 
Protection Agency, 604 U.S. __ (2025), the Supreme Court of the  United States held that the Clean Water Act does 
not authorize the USEPA to impose “end- result” provisions in its discharge permits. The Supreme Court’s decision 
requires the USEPA to follow the Clean Water Act by establishing clear permit requirements that prevent pollution by 
restricting discharges before those discharges reach receiving waters. The decision does not relieve the SFPUC of its 
Clean Water Act responsibilities, but the USEPA’s “end-result” requirements could have resulted in the SFPUC having 
to incur significant, unknowable capital costs, adversely impacting the SFPUC’s ability to achieve its affordability 
goals.  See also “FINANCIAL OPERATIONS – Retail Water Sales Revenue – Affordability.” 
Bay Delta Water Quality Standards 
The Water Enterprise obtains the majority of its Bay Area water supply from Hetch Hetchy Reservoir, located 
on the main branch of the Tuolumne River, which is an upstream tributary to the Lower San Joaquin River and the 
San Francisco Bay/Sacramento San Joaquin Delta Estuary (the “Bay Delta”). 
In 2009, the SWRCB noticed its intent to update water quality objectives of the 2006 Water Quality Control 
Plan for the San Francisco Bay/Sacramento-San Joaquin Delta Estuary and the program of implementation, which had 
the potential to result in impacts to water rights and the issuance of water quality regulations consistent with the 
program of implementation. Following a several years -long process, in 2018, the SWRCB adopted the “Phase 1” 
Amendment to the Water Quality Control Plan  (“2018 Plan Amendment”) and the accompanying final Substitute 
Environmental Document. The adopted 2018 Plan Amendment was developed with the stated goal of increasing 
salmonid populations in three San Joaquin River tributaries (the Stanislaus, Merced, and Tuolumne Rivers) and the 
Bay-Delta. The 2018 Plan Amendment requires the release of 40% (by default, but may be variable between 30% and 
50%) of the “unimpaired flow” on the three San Joaquin River tributaries from February through June in every water 
year type, whether wet, above normal, dry, or critically dry. If the 2018 Plan Amendment were to be implemented, it 
would result in significant water supply shortages for the Hetch Hetchy Regional Water System during single dry and 
multiple dry years. 
The SWRCB has three primary means of implementing the 40% unimpaired flow requirement: (1)  in a 
separate water rights proceeding that would consider any necessary changes to upstream water rights; (2) by adopting 
a regulation and issuing orders on targeted water rights; or (3)  through Clean Water Act section 401 certification 
actions in FERC hydroelectric licensing processes, such as the FERC relicensing proceeding underway for the Don 
Pedro Project on the Tuolumne River (see “ – FERC Proceeding to Increase Flows in the Lower Tuolumne River” 
below). 120 
In 2019, the City joined in filing a lawsuit with the San Joaquin Tributaries Authority challenging the 
SWRCB’s adoption of the 2018 Plan Amendment on multiple legal grounds including the California Environmental 
Quality Act and the Porter Cologne Act. Over a dozen other lawsuits have been filed in State and federal court 
challenging the 2018 Plan Amendment. Through 2021 and 2022, the Sacramento County Superior Court has issued a 
series of rulings in favor of the State again st a number of petitioners, which has led to the dismissal without leave to 
amend of several causes of action, and upholding other causes of action. On March 15, 2024, the Superior Court issued 
a final order on the merits, denying all claims raised by all petitioners and ruling in favor of the SWRCB on all issues 
raised. On May 15, 2024, the San Joaquin Tributaries Authority and nine other petitioners appealed the decision of the 
Superior Court; these appeals were consolidated. These consolidated appeals remain pending and have not yet reached 
the briefing stage. 
The 2018 Plan Amendment further directed the SWRCB to develop biological goals, or targets, to explain 
the expected outcomes of the implementation of the 2018 Plan Amendment and to inform the SWRCB’s exercise of 
the adaptive management provisions of the 2018 Plan Amendment. In July 2023, the SWRCB issued a draft Biological 
Goals document. Commenters, including the San Joaquin Tributaries Authority, raised concerns that the biological 
goals the SWRCB developed were unrelated to, and would not be attainable through, the implementation of the 2018 
Plan Amendments. In September 6, 2023, the SWRCB adopted the Biological Goals without resolving many of the 
concerns that the San Joaquin Tributaries Authority and other parties raised.  On February 6, 2024, the SWRCB 
re-adopted the Biological Goals without substantive change but with the addition of new CEQA findings.  On 
October 6, 2023 and March 5, 2024, respectively, the San Joaquin Tributaries Authority filed suit challenging the 
SWRCB’s two actions adopting the Biological Goals, alleging violations of the California Environmental Quality Act 
and the California Administrative Procedure Act, among others.  These lawsuits remain pending and have not reached 
the merits stage.  
When the SWRCB approved the 2018 Plan Amendment, it also directed staff to compile a Delta watershed-
wide agreement, including potential flow measures for the Tuolumne River, and to incorporate such agreements as an 
alternative for a futur e amendment to the Bay -Delta Water Quality Control Plan to be presented to the SWRCB as 
early as possible after December 1, 2019. On March 1, 2019, in accordance with the SWRCB’s instruction, the SFPUC, 
in partnership with the Modesto Irrigation District and Turlock Irrigation District (collectively, the “ Tuolumne 
Parties”), submitted to the State a proposed voluntary agreement (“ Tuolumne Voluntary Agreement ”). A 
memorandum of understanding to advance the Tuolumne V oluntary Agreement was signed by the Tuolumne Parties 
in November 2022. In spring 2023, the Tuolumne Parties and other public water agencies completed necessary draft 
terms and agreements and submitted them to the SWRCB for review. The public water agencies also renamed the 
“V oluntary Agreements” to the “Healthy Rivers and Landscapes” (“HR&L ”) program. The SWRCB conducted a  
series of workshops in 2023 and 2024 to publicly review the Sacramento portion of the HRL&L program. The SWRCB 
expects to consider the Tuolumne portion of the HR&L program for adoption in 2026. The SWRCB process will 
include (1) scientific peer review of the Tuolumne HR&L program, and (2) release of a draft CEQA document for 
public comment and a final CEQA document. After a final CEQA document is issued, the SWRCB would then be 
able to consider adoption of the Tuolumne HR&L program. If successful, this process may allow the SFPUC to offset 
some of the flow requirement of the 2018 Plan Amendments by agreeing to instead implement certain “non- flow” 
measures designed to improve the viability of targeted fish species. These negotiations remain ongoing. 
In addition, in 2019 and 2020, the State and other parties filed multiple lawsuits against the federal 
government for its adoption of biological opinions that altered how the U.S. Bureau of Reclamation would operate the 
Central Valley Project.  In December 2024, the Bureau of Reclamation issued a record of decision reversing the record 
of decision issued in 2020.  In 2020, several other lawsuits were filed against the State for its issuance and adoption 
of an incidental take permit governing how the California Department of Water Resources would operate the State 
Water Project. While the City is not participating in these lawsuits, this litigation may continue to impact negotiations 
for a Delta watershed-wide agreement. 
FERC Proceeding to Increase Flows in the Lower Tuolumne River 
FERC licenses the Don Pedro Hydropower Project (“ Don Pedro Project ”), owned and operated by the 
Irrigation Districts. The City helped fund the original construction of Don Pedro Project in exchange for a 570,000  
acre-foot “water bank” account allowing the SFPUC to receive water credits for advanced releases from the Hetch 121 
Hetchy Project to the New Don Pedro Reservoir, and to debit from this water bank by diverting water otherwise 
allocated to the Irrigation Districts. 
The current FERC license for the Don Pedro Project expired in 2016, and the Irrigation Districts are now 
operating under an annual license that incorporates the conditions of their prior license. The I rrigation Districts 
initiated the process to relicense the Don Pedro Project using FERC’s Integrated Licensing Process in 2010. 
Relicensing is a lengthy process, stretching over several years and open to public participation. It is estimated the 
process may cost up to $50 million to complete, which costs are split for certain studies between the Irrigation Districts 
and the SFPUC pursuant to an existing agreement. The Irrigation Districts submitted an Amended Final License 
Application (“AFLA”) for the Don Pedro Project on October 11, 2017. 
The Irrigation Districts are also working through an initial licensing proceeding for the La Grange Diversion 
Dam (the “La Grange Project”), which is located on the Tuolumne River, two miles downstream of the Don Pedro 
Project. The Irrigation Districts also submitted their Final License Application (“FLA”) for the La Grange Project on 
October 11, 2017. The City has no legal or contractual responsibilities regarding the ownership or operation of the La 
Grange Project and is not subject to the licensing conditions for the La Grange Project.  
NEPA and CEQA environmental review must be completed before licenses can be issued. On July 7, 2020, 
FERC released the Final Environmental Impact Statement (“FEIS”) for both the AFLA for the Don Pedro Project and 
the FLA for the La Grange Project. The Irrigation Districts are in the process of conducting CEQA review. The FEIS 
presents a “staff recommendation” that is mostly aligned with the Irrigation Districts’ AFLA. 
As described i n the “Bay Delta Water Quality Standards” section above, under section 401 of the federal 
Clean Water Act, FERC cannot issue a new license for a hydroelectric project until a state’s water quality agency 
issues a certification stating that the new license will comply with the applicable water quality requirements (the “401 
Certification”) or, alternatively, FERC determines that the state has waived its authority to issue a 401 Certification. 
The ongoing WQCP voluntary agreement process is designed, in part,  to draft the terms of the 401 Certification that 
would be included as part of the FERC license in lieu of the 40% unimpaired flow requirement set forth in the 2018 
Plan Amendment. However, in January 2021, the SWRCB issued a final 401 Certification that s ets a flow schedule 
more onerous than the 2018 Plan Amendment. The SFPUC and the Irrigation Districts filed petitions for 
reconsideration at the SWRCB in February 2021. The SWRCB did not timely respond to these petitions and the City 
and the Irrigation Districts consequently filed related lawsuits challenging the 401 Certification. On May 7, 2024, the 
SWRCB unilaterally set aside the 401 Certification, and, on August 13, 2024, the court entered the stipulated 
dismissal-without-prejudice of the City’s and the Irrigation Districts’ lawsuits. In addition, the Irrigation Districts 
challenged FERC to find that the SWRCB has waived its authority to issue a 401 Certification. The United States 
Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia Circuit decided the Irriga tion Districts’ waiver lawsuit unfavorably to 
the Irrigation Districts, and, in April 2023, the United States Supreme Court denied the Irrigation Districts’ petition 
for certiorari, fully resolving this case. On December 15, 2024, the Irrigation Districts re -applied for a section 401 
Certification, and that process remains ongoing. 
A 1995 Don Pedro Project Settlement Agreement (“ Settlement Agreement”) and a 1996 Order by FERC 
(“1996 Order”) established increased water flows on the Tuolumne River to protect fisheries and riparian resources. 
A restoration plan (“Restoration Plan”) adopted in 2000 guides planning, funding and implementation efforts. The 
Restoration Plan calls for a series of projects with a combined estimated cost of $25 million to improve river channel, 
riparian, and fisheries conditions within a 27- mile stretch of the Tuolumne River corridor below the La Grange 
Diversion Dam. Four of the ten priority projects were completed with available State and federal funding. More 
recently, the City and the Irrigation Districts as well as other non -governmental organizations have been working on 
implementing additional habitat restoration projects related to the Restoration Plan.  
Pursuant to a 1966 agreement between the City and the Irrigation Districts, the City might have been liable 
to provide a portion of the increased flows mandated under the 1995 Settlement Agreement. Instead, the City and the 
Irrigation Districts entered into a new agreement (the “ Side Agreement”) whereby the Irrigation Districts ag reed to 
provide all flows ordered by FERC to implement the Settlement Agreement until FERC issues a new license for the 
Don Pedro Project in exchange for which the City pays the Irrigation Districts $3.5 million per year, subject to an 
escalation clause ap plied to keep pace with inflation. Pursuant to the terms of its agreement with the Irrigation 
Districts, the City may withdraw from the agreement upon one year’s notice. 122 
The term of the Side Agreement runs until FERC issues a new license for the Don Pedro Project. License 
conditions, such as water-release requirements, could change under a new license. Changed release requirements could 
adversely affect the availability of Tuolumne River water to the SFPUC and hydroelectric generation. To prevent these 
potentially adverse effects to its operations on the Tuolumne River, the SFPUC is seeking to extend the existing Side 
Agreement so that it would remain in effect after the Don Pedro Project FERC license is renewed.  
FERC has not yet provided a schedule for reli censing. However, it is expected that the National Marine 
Fisheries Service will first exercise its Endangered Species Act section 7 consultation authority before FERC moves 
forward with license approval. The National Marine Fisheries Service has not indicated when it will commence this 
consultation process. 
Dam Licensing and Safety Issues 
In 1929, the California Legislature enacted legislation providing for supervision over non- federal dams in 
the State. The statutes place the supervision of the safety of non-federal dams and reservoirs under the jurisdiction of 
the DSOD. Dams under DSOD jurisdiction are artificial barriers, together with appurtenant work, including outlet 
towers, which are 25 feet or more in height or have an impounding capacity of 50 acre-feet or more. Any artificial 
barrier not in excess of six feet in height, regardless of storage, or that has a capacity not in excess of 15 acre-feet, 
regardless of height, is not considered within DSOD’s jurisdiction. 
The DSOD reviews plans and specifications for the construction of new dams or for the enlargement, 
alteration, repair or removal of existing dams, under applications, and must grant written approval before the owner 
can proceed with construction. The DSOD routinely inspects operating dams to ensure that they are adequately 
maintained. The DSOD also conducts investigations of selected dams and directs the owners to additional 
investigations and detailed safety evaluations when necessary. In 2017, Oroville Dam, which is owned and operated 
by the State, suffered significant spillway structural problems and a major downstream area was temporarily 
evacuated. As a result of the Oroville Dam spillway problems, the State Legislature enacted legislation to enhance the 
DSOD’s authority; pursuant to such authority, the DSOD has requested additional evaluations of certain spillways, 
including spillways at the following four dams: Cherry Valley, O’Shaughnessy, Turner, and San Andreas. 
SFPUC Dam and Safety Resiliency Program. The SFPUC has 18 dams under the jurisdiction of the DSOD. 
In 2019, the SFPUC performed a comprehensive overview of evaluations and potential capital improvements that may 
be needed for these dams in the following 15 years. The SFPUC created a proposed schedule for improvement projects 
for 15 of the dams and submitted the schedule to the DSOD in 2020. Several new projects were added to the Capital 
Plan over time to address these needs. In 2024, an updated letter was sent to DSOD sharing progress on the evaluations 
and capital projects for 15 of the dams.  In most cases, additional evaluations are being conducted to further determine 
capital needs as part of each project’s planning phase. Thus, while estimated funding has been included in the 2026 
Capital Plan for these facility improvements, the scopes, budgets and schedules may be adjusted in future years as 
more information becomes available.  
In December 2023, the SFPUC submitted a pre-application to the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers for a CWIFP 
loan to fi nance a collection of eight Water Enterprise projects supporting dam safety and resilience. In September 
2024, the U .S. Army Corps of E ngineers notified the SFPUC that its pre-application was selected to submit a full 
application for CWIFP financing consideration for a loan up to $614,811,376.  The SFPUC plans to submit the full 
application prior to the deadline of September 9, 2025.   For more information regarding the CWIFP loan, see 
“OBLIGATIONS PAYABLE FROM REVENUES – Corps Water Infrastructure Financing Program.” 
Capital improvement projects for SFPUC’s dams that are currently funded under the 2026 Capital Plan  are 
described below: 
O’Shaughnessy Dam. O’Shaughnessy Dam was completed in 1923 and raised in 1938. Most of the outlet 
facilities were installed when the dam was first built in 1923. These outlet facilities have exceeded their design life 
and need rehabilitation or replacement to maintain their reliability and functionality. The O’Shaughnessy Dam Outlet 
Works Project will extend the service life and reliable operation of the 100- year-old outlet system. The rehabilitation 
work is divided into two phases. The scope of Phase 1 includes replacing the access platforms in ladder wells, clearing 
critical drainage system s within the dam, building a new bulkhead system as a safe isolation for upcoming 123 
rehabilitation of gates and valves, replacing the instream flow release valves, and pre- construction planning of drum 
gates and slide gates replacement. The value of construction work for Phase 1 is es timated to be over $30 million 
dollars. Phase 1 construction has started with anticipated construction completion in 2026. The objective of Phase 2 
is to rehabilitate slide gates, drum gates, large valves such as the 60-inch and 72-inch diameter needle valves, diversion 
tunnel and diversion pipeline, and associated control systems. The value of construction work for Phase 2 is estimated 
to be over $90 million dollars. The planning of Phase 2 will start in 2025, with staggered construction starting in 2030 
and anticipated construction completion in 2041. The current estimated total project cost  (which includes planning, 
bid and award, and close out costs, as well as construction costs)  is $44 million for Phase 1 and $184 million for 
Phase 2. 
Lake Eleanor Dam. Lake Eleanor Dam is a multiple arch reinforced concrete dam with roadway bridge at 
the crest that was constructed in 1918. Due to the age of the dam, some of the structural elements including the bridge 
and concrete barrels exhibit various degree s of deterioration including cracking and spalling of concrete, exposed 
rebar, leakage through the arch barrels, and cracks and erosion of the spillway concrete. The bridge is at the top of the 
dam and is an integral structure of the dam, providing the only vehicle  and personnel access to the spillway structure 
and the other side of the dam for maintenance purposes. In addition, the dam’s spillway is inadequate to pass the 
updated inflow design flood. The Lake Eleanor Dam Rehabilitation Project will address concerns  with concrete 
deterioration and spill capacity of the existing dam concrete structure, and first, rehabilitate the bridge, then 
subsequently replace the bridge deck when overall dam improvements are constructed. The project’s first phase, the 
bridge rehabilitation project, is in the final design phase with anticipated construction completion in 2027 and an 
estimated construction cost of about $4 million. The second phase of the project will address dam improvements 
including concrete deficiencies, increased capacity for releases, and increased downstream erosion protection. 
Planning for this second phase is funded to start in 2025, with anticipated construction completion in 2037. The 
estimated total project cost for both phases of improvements to the bridge and dam is $114 million.  
Priest Dam.   The Priest Dam Condition Assessment and Monitoring Project includes a geotechnical 
exploration of the dam, a stability analysis, and a complete Condition Assessment Report. Additionally, the project 
will install new instrumentation to monitor the dam’s performance. Pre -construction work (review of files, records, 
and site visits) started in 2022 with the geotechnical investigation commencing in 2024 due to the need to coordinate 
with the Mountain Tunnel Project shutdo wns. Construction work has been completed and the Condition Assessment 
Report is currently in progress. The estimated project cost is $1.5-2.5 million. Pending the results from the Priest Dam 
Condition Assessment in 2025-2026, it is expected that a Priest Dam rehabilitation project may be developed in the 
future to correct potential deficiencies. 
Cherry Valley Dam. Cherry Valley Dam was built in 1955. The spillway includes a 334-foot wide ogee-type 
concrete weir that discharges into an unlined adjacent chan nel. Engineering studies showed that remedial measures 
and erosion protection for the spill channel are needed to ensure that spill flows from Cherry Valley Dam spillway can 
be contained without erosion damage to the existing embankment dam and downstream area. Studies also found that 
long-term improvement to the spillway is needed to increase the hydraulic capacity of the spillway to safely pass the 
updated inflow design flood. The 2026 Capital Plan includes Cherry Dam Spillway Short-Term Improvements project 
as a short-term interim solution until a project for long-term spillway improvements can be implemented.  The dam’s 
original design documents indicate that the spillway is rated for 52,000 cubic feet per second (“ cfs”). However, 
significant erosion damage occurred below the dam in 2010 when corresponding spillway releases were less than 
1,800 cfs. The Cherry Dam Spillway Short -Term Improvements project will minimize the potential spill damage by 
improving the portion of the spillway that is unlined with erosion protection measures and
 re-establishing containment 
for the breached spill channel section from the 2010 spill, improving the flood protection for the dam and downstream 
area under a normal spill event.  The value of construction work is estimated to be $8 million with anticipated 
construction completion in 2027; the total project cost is $15 million. The SFPUC also plans to include a long- term 
project in the future capital plan to increase the spillway capacity and retrofit the intake tower for seismic reliability.  
Early Intake Dam. Early Intake Dam is a single-curvature concrete arch structure. It was constructed between 
1923 and 1924 to divert Hetch Hetchy water from the Tuolumne River into the Mountain Tunnel. Within a few years 
of the dam’s completion, significant cracking was observed at several locations, most likely due to the alkali-aggregate 
reactivity in the concrete. The structure has continued to deteriorate since then. Widely distributed and progressively 
worsening cracking has been observed throughout the dam and spillway structure over the years. The dam is reaching 
the end of its design life. The SFPUC has initiated a project to replace the dam. The Early Intake Dam Long -term 124 
Improvement Project will remove the existing concrete stru cture and construct a new facility to allow diversion of 
flows into Mountain Tunnel for water delivery under emergency and drought conditions. The SFPUC is considering 
a wide variety of alternatives, including, but not limited to, using pumps, low head weirs, tunnels, pipelines, and other 
water sources to supplement water supply to the Bay Area assuming the dam is removed. It is expected to take six 
years, from 2024 to 2029, to resolve complex environmental issues, water supply reliability issues, and obtai n 
necessary approvals. The cost of construction work is estimated to be over $50 million dollars. The planning phase of 
the project has started, with anticipated construction completion in 2034.  
Moccasin Lower Dam . In 2018, the town of Moccasin experienced more than 5.0 inches of precipitation 
over a 24 -hour period, of which 3.1 inches fell within a four -hour period. The torrent of rain caused severe flood 
damage to the facilities in Moccasin and almost overtopped Moccasin Dam. After the event, the SFPUC i ssued 
emergency contracts to remove debris and perform emergency interim repairs. All of the emergency repairs were 
completed in 2020. To ensure dam safety and minimize potential flood damage in the future, the SFPUC re-evaluated 
the required flood protection for the dam and downstream facilities. The scope of work for the Moccasin Dam Long-
Term Improvements project includes re-constructing the auxiliary spillway to handle the updated inflow design flood 
and building additional measures to protect the major facilities. The project will consist of constructing a new ungated 
side channel spillway at the right abutment along the alignment of the existing auxiliary spill channel. Additional 
erosion protection will also be considered for both upstream and downstream of the reservoir. Planning began in 2020 
to define the scope of the spillway improvements; construction is anticipated to occur in 2030-2033 to allow sufficient 
time for planning, engineering design, and environmental agency approvals. The value of construction work is 
estimated to be over $100 million dollars with an estimated total project cost of $142 million, and anticipated 
construction completion in 2033.  
Pilarcitos Dam. Pilarcitos Dam is a 95-foot-high earth fill embankment dam originally constructed in 1866 
by the Spring Valley Water Company, with a reservoir capacity of approximately 3,100 acre-feet. An overall condition 
and needs assessment of the facility was completed in 2021. The study identified that the embankment and the  
appurtenant structures have operational and dam safety (seismic and hydraulic) improvement needs. The scope of 
work for Pilarcitos Dam includes upgrades to the embankment, outlet structure, and spillway to meet seismic safety 
standards and to pass the updated inflow design flood. Conceptual engineering design for two alternatives, 
representing the highest and lowest capital costs, is currently underway. Pre -construction is scheduled to complete in 
2031 with construction scheduled for completion in 2034. The estimated project budget is $64 million.   
San Andreas Dam . San Andreas Dam is a 105 -foot-high earth fill embankment dam built in 1870 and 
modified in 1875. The dam impounds a 19,000- acre-foot reservoir in San Mateo County. An overall condition and 
needs assessment for the dam was completed in 2021, identifying the need to improve and upgrade the embankment 
and emergency outlet release facilities along with the spillway, where improvement needs  were previously identified 
in 2019. Preliminary scope of work at San Andreas Dam includes the construction of a new spillway, a seismic stability 
study of the embankment, and a feasibility study of an emergency release outlet structure. Based on the condition 
assessment results, seismic safety standards, and the nee d to pass the updated inflow design flood, the dam’s 
embankment, spillway, and emergency release outlet structure would all require retrofit and/or replacement under one 
construction contract. Alternatives analysis is currently underway. Once completed, scope of work, schedule and 
budget will be updated. Pre -construction for the entire project is scheduled to be complete in 2031 with construction 
scheduled for completion in 2033. The current estimated project budget is $32 million, but this may be increased after 
project needs are further determined. 
Turner Dam. Turner Dam is a 195 -foot-high earth fill embankment dam constructed in 1965. The dam 
impounds the 50,500-acre-foot San Antonio Reservoir in Alameda County. An overall condition and needs assessment 
for the facility, including the embankment, spillway and outlet works, began in 2020 and is part of a project to perform 
necessary upgrades anticipated to be completed by 2034. Results from the latest condition assessment, including an 
analysis performed in  2023, indicate that the dam embankment, spillway, and outlet works would all need to be 
retrofitted and/or replaced to meet the seismic safety standard and to pass the updated inflow design flood. Since this 
project is still in the early planning phase, the schedule and budget for the upgrade project will be updated when the 
scope is better defined for the selected alternative. The project is preliminarily scheduled to complete pre-construction 
in 2031, with construction from 2032 to 2034.  125 
Calaveras Reser voir Expansion. C ompleted in 2019, the Calaveras Dam has a reservoir capacity of 
96,800 acre-feet and was designed to accommodate a potential future enlargement. This project was intended to add 
up to an additional 289,000 acre -feet of additional water sto rage to the Calaveras Dam that could be available in all 
water year types. This project included raising the dam, expanding the capacity of the outlet structures and spillway, 
and adding necessary transmission and pumping infrastructure to convey water to the Calaveras Reservoir. This project 
has been deferred due to, among other reasons, high capital cost requirements, and may be reevaluated in the future.  
See “CAPITAL IMPROVEMENT PROGRAM AND OTHER WATER SUPPLY INITIATIVES  – Water Supply 
Initiatives – AWS Program – AWS Projects – Calaveras Reservoir Expansion (Deferred).” 
Merced Manor Reservoir. The Merced Manor Reservoir project will seismically strengthen and repair the 
roof of the reservoir. Scope of the project includes structural evaluation of the existing roof structure per current 
seismic code, design for seismic strengthening and repair, and construction of improvements. Pre -construction is 
underway with construction completion anticipated by 2031. The total project budget in the 2026 Capital Plan  is 
$12 million. 
Summit Reservoir. Summit Reservoir is a 14 million -gallon (43 acre-feet) capacity potable water storage 
reservoir on Twin Peaks in San Francisco that was built during the 1950s. Based on the as-built drawings, the original 
inlet/outlet structure appeared to be backfilled with compacted fill, whereas the majority of the reservoir is constructed 
on bedrock. Following the WSIP-funded seismic improvements, roof strengthening, and installation of new inlet/outlet 
structure that was completed in 2012, the DSOD reviewed the reservoir improvements and concluded that the reservoir 
met the criteria to be under their jurisdiction as a “dam” due to the original outlet structure embankment. In 2017, at 
the request of the DSOD, an engineering evaluation was performed in order to analyze the subsurface conditions and 
confirm that the seismic improvements performed under the WSIP were adequate, particularly in the area of 
embankment fill where the original inlet-outlet facilities were constructed. In 2019, a geotechnical investigation report 
was submitted to the DSOD that provided additional test results and information about the subsurface materials and 
conditions and concluded that the ground and the embankment appear to be stable.  In 2021, an inspecti on of the 
concrete floor and walls in the West basin showed degradation of the sealant joints as well as some cracking and 
spalling along the floor and walls. Pre -construction of the repairs will be completed in 2025 and the repair of the 
cracks will be performed in 2026-2027, with similar repairs to any potential cracks in the East basin thereafter.  Total 
project cost is less than $2 million; additional budget will be requested in the future if needed.  
Stanford Heights Reservoir . Stanford Heights Reservoir, a potable water storage reservoir located within 
San Francisco, is impounded by a 31- foot-high earth fill dam constructed in 1928. The SFPUC performed a stability 
analysis in 1993, and an updated seismic stability and deformation analysis check of the embankment in 1997. Further 
geotechnical and seismic stability investigations are needed to determine if improvements may be necessary. Further 
investigations and pre-construction activities are planned for 2026 to 2027, with potential construction, if needed, in 
2028 to 2030.  
Sunset Reservoir South Basin. In a 1996 study, the SFPUC recommended seismic strengthening of the roof 
structure and other associated reservoir structural elements at the Sunset Reservoir South Basin which is formed with 
a 34-foot-high earth fill dam within San  Francisco. However, due to budgetary prioritization decisions made by the 
SFPUC, and because the North Basin was seismically retrofitted, those recommendations were deferred.  In 2018, a 
structural and seismic performance review of the Sunset South Basin recommended updating the seismic evaluation 
of the roof structure and structural elements prior to performing a structural improvement project. A project to 
strengthen the reservoir roof structure and other structural components  if needed is scheduled for pre -construction in 
2026 to 2030 with construction completion in 2033.  
University Mound South Basin. In a 1996 study, the SFPUC recommended seismic strengthening of the roof 
structure and other associated reservoir structural elements at the University Mound South Basin which is formed with 
a 17-foot-high earth fill dam within San  Francisco. However, due to budgetary prioritization decisions, and because 
the North Basin was seismically retrofitted, those recommendations were deferred. A seismic stability and deformation 
analysis of the embankment conducted by DSOD in 2006 showed acceptable seismic performance of the embankment. 
Thus, it is anticipated that the improvement project will only be needed to seismically strengthen the roof structure 
and structural components of the reservoir. Pre -construction including planning is scheduled for 2026 to 2030, with 
improvements constructed by 2033.  126 
Hazardous Material Management 
The handling of hazardous materials is subject to a variety of federal and State regulations. The SFPUC 
currently complies with regulations regarding hazardous material safety with respect to hazardous material disposal 
and employee safety. 
Endangered Species 
Various species (including native fishes) present in and near the Tuolumne River and Bay Area streams (e.g., 
Alameda, San Mateo and Pilarcitos Creeks) are either listed or candidates for listing under the State or federal 
endangered species acts. New listings and future regulatory actions under the acts, or con ditions placed in permits to 
undertake construction for certain capital projects, could potentially directly affect water supplies available to the 
Regional Water System. When necessary, the SFPUC works with the responsible State and federal agencies to obtain 
permits under the acts. 
Required Instream Flow Schedules from Regional Water System Dams 
In order to comply with federal and State permit requirements in connection with dam and reservoir 
improvements to be carried out as part of the WSIP, the SFPUC has implemented schedules of instream flow releases 
from Lower Crystal Springs Reservoir to San Mateo Creek, from Calaveras Reservoir to Calaveras Creek/Alameda 
Creek, and from the Upper Alameda Creek Diversion Dam to Alameda Creek to enhance habitat for native species.  
The SFPUC is constructing the Alameda Creek Recapture Project to recover a portion of the loss of water supply 
associated with Calaveras Dam releases and bypasses.
